items.body:
**Summary**
When generating CMP Certificate Signing Requests (CSRs) using OpenSSL tooling or library functions, it is currently difficult to inspect the exact contents of an outgoing CSR unless it is explicitly written to a file and manually decoded afterwards. This feature request proposes adding enhanced debugging support that allows CSR contents to be printed to the console (or debug output) when a certain verbosity or debug level is enabled.
This would significantly improve debuggability and transparency for developers and operators working with automated PKI workflows, enrollment protocols, or custom OpenSSL integrations.

**Motivation**
In many real-world scenarios, CSRs are generated programmatically and sent directly to a CA. When something goes wrong—such as:

- malformed subject information
- unexpected SAN entries
- missing extensions
- incorrect key usage
- CA rejection with cryptic error messages

developers currently have limited visibility into what exactly was sent unless additional manual steps are taken.
Being able to inspect the CSR content inline during execution would:

- reduce debugging time
- simplify troubleshooting in CI/CD pipelines
- help validate dynamically generated CSR parameters
- improve observability without requiring temporary files
items.title: Improve CMP client-side debugging: optional console cutput of CSR Contents

There are now 2 `configuration.h` files. Without carefull ordering of `-I` paths, the compilation of
`apps/list.c` could pick up `include/openssl/configuration.h` instead.
 
So I respectfully urge you to rename this to `apps/include/app_configuration.h` or include it as
`#include "apps/include/configuration.h"` instead.

Hi,

We have an implementation of encrypt-decrypt using OpenSSL APIs.
The code was working fine with OpenSSL version 3.0.14.
But it fails when we upgrade to OpenSSL version 3.5.5.

To be specific, we are seeing the EVP_PKEY_encrypt() API to fail.

Below is a basic code snippet showing the OpenSSL APIs used in the application and the order in which they are called:
```
EVP_PKEY_CTX* ctx = EVP_PKEY_CTX_new(publicKey, NULL);
EVP_PKEY_encrypt_init(ctx);
EVP_PKEY_CTX_set_rsa_padding(ctx, RSA_PKCS1_OAEP_PADDING);
EVP_PKEY_encrypt(ctx, outBuffer, &outLen, charArray, fileSize);
```


Can you help us understand what changed between the version that might be causing the issue?
Also, any fix suggestion is highly appreciated.

Thanks in advance.

Regards,
Amal

items.body:
# duplicate extension type handling in OpenSSL

## 1. Conclusion

The standard requires that the same extension type must not appear more than once in one extension block.

OpenSSL only enforces this strictly for extension types that it recognizes:

- built-in extensions: duplicate is rejected
- registered custom extensions: duplicate is rejected
- unknown extensions: accepted/ignored at collection time, so duplicate unknown types are not rejected by the duplicate check itself

## 2. Standard English description

The relevant normative text is from [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 4.2](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.2):

```text
When multiple extensions of different types are present, the
extensions MAY appear in any order, with the exception of
"pre_shared_key" (Section 4.2.11) which MUST be the last extension in
the ClientHello (but can appear anywhere in the ServerHello
extensions block).  There MUST NOT be more than one extension of the
same type in a given extension block.
```

The key normative sentence is:

```text
There MUST NOT be more than one extension of the same type in a given extension block.
```

This requirement is global. It does not say "known extension types only". It says `the same type`, which means the rule should apply to all extension types appearing in that block.

## 3. Code description

### 3.1 OpenSSL explicitly documents its own implementation boundary

In `ssl/statem/extensions.c`, the comment above `tls_collect_extensions()` already states the real behavior:

```c
/*
 * Per http://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc5246#section-7.4.1.4, there may not be
 * more than one extension of the same type in a ClientHello or ServerHello.
 * This function returns 1 if all extensions are unique and we have parsed their
 * types, and 0 if the extensions contain duplicates, could not be successfully
 * found, or an internal error occurred. We only check duplicates for
 * extensions that we know about. We ignore others.
 */
```

This is the most direct evidence for the inconsistency.

The important sentence is:

```c
We only check duplicates for extensions that we know about. We ignore others.
```

So OpenSSL itself is telling us that its duplicate detection is intentionally limited.

### 3.2 Unknown extensions are allowed in `verify_extension()`

The collection logic depends on `verify_extension()`. Its final branch is:

```c
    /* Unknown extension. We allow it */
    *found = NULL;
    return 1;
```

This means:

1. if the extension is built-in, OpenSSL finds a slot for it
2. if it is a registered custom extension, OpenSSL also finds a slot for it
3. if it is unknown, OpenSSL still returns success, but `*found` is set to `NULL`

So unknown extensions are not rejected here. They are admitted into the parsing flow as "allowed but untracked".

### 3.3 Duplicate rejection depends on `thisex->present`

The actual duplicate check in `tls_collect_extensions()` is:

```c
        if (!verify_extension(s, context, type, exts, raw_extensions, &thisex)
            || (thisex != NULL && thisex->present == 1)
            || (type == TLSEXT_TYPE_psk
                && (context & SSL_EXT_CLIENT_HELLO) != 0
                && PACKET_remaining(&extensions) != 0)) {
            SSLfatal(s, SSL_AD_ILLEGAL_PARAMETER, SSL_R_BAD_EXTENSION);
            goto err;
        }
```

The key condition is:

```c
(thisex != NULL && thisex->present == 1)
```

This means duplicate detection only works when:

- the extension has been mapped to an internal slot, and
- that slot was already marked as present before

If the extension is unknown, then `verify_extension()` sets:

```c
*found = NULL;
```

So for unknown extensions:

```c
thisex == NULL
```

and therefore the duplicate condition does not trigger.

### 3.4 Why known extensions are rejected but unknown ones are not

The reason is structural:

- OpenSSL stores duplicate state in `RAW_EXTENSION` slots
- only recognized built-in or registered custom extensions get such a slot
- unknown extensions do not get a slot
- no slot means no `present` bit
- no `present` bit means no duplicate hit

So the implementation is not "raw extension type de-duplication". It is "de-duplication over recognized extension table entries".

## 4. Why this is inconsistent with the standard

The standard ([RFC 8446, Section 4.2](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.2)) says:

```text
There MUST NOT be more than one extension of the same type in a given extension block.
```

But OpenSSL effectively implements:

- no more than one recognized extension of the same type
- unknown extension types are ignored by the duplicate checker

That is narrower than the standard requirement.

So the inconsistency is:

- standard scope: all extension types in the block
- OpenSSL scope: only extension types that OpenSSL knows how to index internally

## 5. Final reason for 

- it correctly rejects duplicates for known extensions
- it does not enforce the same rule for unknown extension types

In other words, OpenSSL satisfies the rule on a recognized subset of extension types, but not on the full set required by the TLS 1.3 text.

labels.description: The issue is not considered a bug
labels.name: resolved: not a bug

items.title: Duplicate extension type handling in OpenSSL

items.body:
# OpenSSL unknown extension handling split analysis

## 1. Conclusion

This issue should be analyzed separately for the server side and the client side.

- Server side: when OpenSSL receives unknown extensions in `ClientHello`, it follows the TLS 1.3 compatibility rule and ignores them. This is consistent with RFC 8446.
- Client side: when OpenSSL receives unknown extensions in `ServerHello`, it is strict for recognized extensions, but fully unknown extension types are still admitted by the generic collection logic and are not uniformly rejected at the extension parsing stage. This is weaker than the RFC 8446 `ServerHello` constraint.

Therefore, this item is best classified as `partial`, not `satisfied`.

## 2. Standard English description

### 2.1 Server-side rule: unknown extensions in `ClientHello`

The relevant normative text is from [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 4.1.2](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.1.2):

```text
extensions:  Clients request extended functionality from servers by
   sending data in the extensions field.  The actual "Extension"
   format is defined in Section 4.2.  In TLS 1.3, the use of certain
   extensions is mandatory, as functionality has moved into
   extensions to preserve ClientHello compatibility with previous
   versions of TLS.  Servers MUST ignore unrecognized extensions.
```

The key sentence is:

```text
Servers MUST ignore unrecognized extensions.
```

This sentence applies to the server receiving `ClientHello`.

### 2.2 Client-side rule: extension set in `ServerHello`

The relevant normative text is from [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 4.1.3](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.1.3):

```text
extensions:  A list of extensions.  The ServerHello MUST only include
   extensions which are required to establish the cryptographic
   context and negotiate the protocol version.  All TLS 1.3
   ServerHello messages MUST contain the "supported_versions"
   extension.  Current ServerHello messages additionally contain
   either the "pre_shared_key" extension or the "key_share"
   extension, or both (when using a PSK with (EC)DHE key
   establishment).  Other extensions (see Section 4.2) are sent
   separately in the EncryptedExtensions message.
```

The core requirement is:

```text
The ServerHello MUST only include extensions which are required to establish the cryptographic context and negotiate the protocol version.
```

The request/response rule from [RFC 8446, Section 4.2](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.2) is:

```text
Implementations MUST NOT send extension responses if the remote
endpoint did not send the corresponding extension requests, with the
exception of the "cookie" extension in the HelloRetryRequest.  Upon
receiving such an extension, an endpoint MUST abort the handshake
with an "unsupported_extension" alert.
```

And the alert definition from [RFC 8446, Section 6.2](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-6.2) says:

```text
unsupported_extension:  Sent by endpoints receiving any handshake
   message containing an extension known to be prohibited for
   inclusion in the given handshake message, or including any
   extensions in a ServerHello or Certificate not first offered in
   the corresponding ClientHello or CertificateRequest.
```

Together, these rules mean that `ServerHello` is not a general "unknown extension can always be ignored" context. It is a tightly constrained response message.

## 3. OpenSSL code strategy

### 3.1 Shared collection logic

In `ssl/statem/extensions.c`, OpenSSL uses a shared extension collector for both directions.

The first important behavior is in `verify_extension()`:

```c
static int verify_extension(SSL_CONNECTION *s, unsigned int context,
    unsigned int type, custom_ext_methods *meths,
    RAW_EXTENSION *rawexlist, RAW_EXTENSION **found)
{
    ...

    /* Unknown extension. We allow it */
    *found = NULL;
    return 1;
}
```

This means that an unknown extension is not rejected immediately. Instead:

- recognized built-in extensions get an internal slot
- registered custom extensions get an internal slot
- unknown extensions are accepted, but `*found` becomes `NULL`

The second important behavior is in `tls_collect_extensions()`:

```c
if (!verify_extension(s, context, type, exts, raw_extensions, &thisex)
    || (thisex != NULL && thisex->present == 1)
    || (type == TLSEXT_TYPE_psk
        && (context & SSL_EXT_CLIENT_HELLO) != 0
        && PACKET_remaining(&extensions) != 0)) {
    SSLfatal(s, SSL_AD_ILLEGAL_PARAMETER, SSL_R_BAD_EXTENSION);
    goto err;
}

...

if (idx < OSSL_NELEM(ext_defs)
    && (context & (SSL_EXT_CLIENT_HELLO
                   | SSL_EXT_TLS1_3_CERTIFICATE_REQUEST
                   | SSL_EXT_TLS1_3_NEW_SESSION_TICKET)) == 0
    ...
    && (s->ext.extflags[idx] & SSL_EXT_FLAG_SENT) == 0) {
    SSLfatal(s, SSL_AD_UNSUPPORTED_EXTENSION,
        SSL_R_UNSOLICITED_EXTENSION);
    goto err;
}
```

The key boundary is:

- duplicate and unsolicited-response checks are mainly applied to extensions that have been indexed into the built-in table
- fully unknown extensions do not get such a slot
- therefore they do not naturally flow through the same strict built-in rejection path

So the shared collector is fundamentally compatibility-oriented for unknown extension types.

### 3.2 Server-side OpenSSL strategy

The server processes `ClientHello` extensions in `ssl/statem/statem_srvr.c`:

```c
extensions = clienthello->extensions;
if (!tls_collect_extensions(s, &extensions, SSL_EXT_CLIENT_HELLO,
        &clienthello->pre_proc_exts,
        &clienthello->pre_proc_exts_len, 1)) {
    /* SSLfatal already been called */
    goto err;
}
```

Because `ClientHello` uses the shared collector with `SSL_EXT_CLIENT_HELLO`, and because unknown extensions are accepted in `verify_extension()`, the practical strategy is:

- accept the raw extension block
- ignore extension types that are not recognized
- continue handshake processing

This matches the RFC 8446 sentence ([Section 4.1.2](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.1.2)):

```text
Servers MUST ignore unrecognized extensions.
```

### 3.3 Client-side OpenSSL strategy

The client processes `ServerHello` extensions in `ssl/statem/statem_clnt.c`:

```c
if (!tls_collect_extensions(s, &extpkt,
        SSL_EXT_TLS1_2_SERVER_HELLO
            | SSL_EXT_TLS1_3_SERVER_HELLO,
        &extensions, NULL, 1)) {
    goto err;
}

...

context = SSL_CONNECTION_IS_TLS13(s) ? SSL_EXT_TLS1_3_SERVER_HELLO
                                     : SSL_EXT_TLS1_2_SERVER_HELLO;
if (!tls_validate_all_contexts(s, context, extensions)) {
    SSLfatal(s, SSL_AD_ILLEGAL_PARAMETER, SSL_R_BAD_EXTENSION);
    goto err;
}
```

For recognized extensions, this is strict:

- the extension must be legal for the message context
- the extension must pass request/response and semantic checks
- illegal recognized extensions can trigger `bad_extension`, `illegal_parameter`, or `unsupported_extension`

But for fully unknown extension types, the path is weaker:

- `verify_extension()` still allows them
- they do not get a normal indexed slot
- `tls_validate_all_contexts()` only iterates over collected indexed extensions
- the built-in unsolicited check is tied to built-in extension indexing

So the client-side strategy is effectively:

- strict for recognized `ServerHello` extensions
- compatibility-oriented for fully unknown `ServerHello` extension types

That is exactly why this item is not `satisfied`.

## 4. Tests we performed

### 4.1 Server-side test: unknown extension in `ClientHello`

We already had runtime evidence in `runtime_retest/id252_unknown_extension_runtime/`.

The server log shows that OpenSSL server received an unknown client extension:

```text
TLS client extension "unknown" (id=65000), len=0
...
SSL_accept:SSLv3/TLS read client hello
SSL_accept:SSLv3/TLS write server hello
...
SSL_accept:SSLv3/TLS write finished
```

The client log shows that the handshake still completed:

```text
CONNECTION ESTABLISHED
Protocol version: TLSv1.3
Ciphersuite: TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384
DONE
```

This test supports the conclusion that OpenSSL server ignores unknown `ClientHello` extensions, which is consistent with RFC 8446.

### 4.2 Client-side test: unknown extension in `ServerHello`

For the client side, we built a runtime test in `runtime_retest/id271_serverhello_unknown_runtime/`.

The test idea was:

1. use a local proxy to modify the first `ClientHello`
2. inject a previously absent empty extension type `0x1234`
3. let the server echo the same extension in `ServerHello`
4. observe whether the OpenSSL client rejects it at `ServerHello` parsing time

The proxy script used for the test was:

```python
UNKNOWN_EXT = b"\\x12\\x34\\x00\\x00"

...

patched, injected = inject_unknown_extension_into_clienthello(buf)
server.sendall(patched)
log(f"injected_unknown_ext={1 if injected else 0}")
```

The proxy log confirms that the extension injection really happened:

```text
proxy_listen=127.0.0.1:44341
client_connected=127.0.0.1:51616
server_connected=127.0.0.1:44340
injected_unknown_ext=1
proxy_done=1
```

The client runtime log shows that the resulting `ServerHello` really contained the unknown extension:

```text
<<< TLS 1.3, Handshake [length 04be], ServerHello
...
28 50 e9 da de 21 39 13 02 00 04 72 12 34 00 00
00 2b 00 02 03 04 00 33 ...
```

Here, `12 34 00 00` is exactly the injected empty extension.

The important observation is that the client did **not** immediately fail with `unsupported_extension` or `illegal_parameter` at the `ServerHello` extension parsing step.

Instead, the later failure was:

```text
>>> TLS 1.3, Alert [length 0002], fatal bad_record_mac
SSL3 alert write:fatal:bad record mac
```

This later `bad_record_mac` is expected in this proxy experiment, because the proxy changed the original `ClientHello`, which changes the handshake transcript and eventually breaks authenticated record processing.

So this client-side test does not mean "the entire handshake succeeds with unknown `ServerHello` extensions". What it does show is:

- the unknown extension was really present in `ServerHello`
- the client did not uniformly reject it at the `ServerHello` extension validation stage
- the failure happened later for transcript/authentication reasons

That is strong evidence for a compatibility-oriented unknown-extension path on the client side.

## 5. Why the inconsistency exists

The mismatch comes from three layers.

### 5.1 RFC 8446 is asymmetric

The standard treats the two directions differently:

- server receiving `ClientHello` ([Section 4.1.2](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.1.2)): ignore unknown extensions
- client receiving `ServerHello` ([Section 4.1.3](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.1.3)): only a narrow set of extensions is allowed, and unsolicited responses should be aborted

So the standard is intentionally not symmetric here.

### 5.2 OpenSSL uses a shared generic collector

OpenSSL reuses one generic extension collection framework for both sides. That framework is intentionally permissive for unknown extension types:

```c
/* Unknown extension. We allow it */
```

This makes server-side compatibility easy, but it also means client-side `ServerHello` handling inherits part of the same permissive behavior.

### 5.3 Strictness is centered on recognized extensions

OpenSSL's strong checks are table-driven:

- recognized built-in extensions
- registered custom extensions

Unknown extension types sit outside that indexed structure. As a result:

- recognized illegal extensions are rejected strictly
- fully unknown extension types do not always hit the same strict rejection path

So the implementation is not "strict for all `ServerHello` extensions". It is "strict for recognized `ServerHello` extensions, compatibility-oriented for unknown ones".

## 6. Final assessment

- server side: behavior is consistent with TLS 1.3
- client side: behavior is only partially consistent, because unknown `ServerHello` extensions are not uniformly rejected at the extension parsing stage

items.title: OpenSSL unknown extension handling split analysis

items.body:
# OfferedPsks binders-identities correspondence gap

## 1. Summary

This issue checks whether `binders` and `identities` are enforced as strict one-to-one pairs in TLS 1.3 `pre_shared_key` parsing.

RFC 8446 describes `binders` as one value for each identity and in the same order.
In the current OpenSSL server parsing path, the implementation validates the binder for the selected identity, but does not add a unified hard-fail check that total binder count must equal total identity count.


## 2. RFC 8446 English Source Text

### 2.1 OfferedPsks structure

From [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 4.2.11](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.2.11):

```text
opaque PskBinderEntry<32..255>;

struct {
    PskIdentity identities<7..2^16-1>;
    PskBinderEntry binders<33..2^16-1>;
} OfferedPsks;
```

### 2.2 One-to-one and same-order requirement

From [RFC 8446, Section 4.2.11](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.2.11):

```text
binders:  A series of HMAC values, one for each value in the
   identities list and in the same order, computed as described
   below.
```

This is the key semantic rule: one binder per identity, same order.

### 2.3 Server validation requirement

From [RFC 8446, Section 4.2.11](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.2.11):

```text
Prior to accepting PSK key establishment, the server MUST validate
the corresponding binder value ...
If this value is not present or does not validate, the server MUST abort the
handshake.  Servers SHOULD NOT attempt to validate multiple binders;
rather, they SHOULD select a single PSK and validate solely the
binder that corresponds to that PSK.
```

Important points:

1. The server MUST validate the corresponding binder for the selected PSK.
2. The server SHOULD NOT validate multiple binders.

### 2.4 Invalid-message handling rule

From [RFC 8446, Section 6](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-6):

```text
Peers which receive a message which is syntactically correct but
semantically invalid ... MUST terminate the connection with an
"illegal_parameter" alert.
```

## 3. OpenSSL Code Path Description

Main function: `tls_parse_ctos_psk()` in `ssl/statem/extensions_srvr.c`.

### 3.1 Parse `identities`

```c
if (!PACKET_get_length_prefixed_2(pkt, &identities)) { ... }
for (id = 0; PACKET_remaining(&identities) != 0; id++) { ... }
```

### 3.2 Parse `binders` and consume only to selected `id`

```c
if (!PACKET_get_length_prefixed_2(pkt, &binders)) { ... }
for (i = 0; i <= id; i++) {
    if (!PACKET_get_length_prefixed_1(&binders, &binder)) { ... }
}
```

### 3.3 Validate selected binder length

```c
if (PACKET_remaining(&binder) != (size_t)hashsize) { ... }
```

### 3.4 Missing global equality check in this path

There is no explicit hard check in this code segment equivalent to:

```text
binders_count == identities_count
```

Therefore, extra binders can remain unconsumed when the selected identity is early in the list.

## 4. Re-Test Evidence

Runtime output from `runtime_retest/id303/test_psk_identities_binders_correspondence.out`:

```text
[A_ids2_binders1_selected0] parse_ok=1 identities=2 selected_id=0 binders_consumed=1 binders_remain=0
[A_ids2_binders1_selected1] parse_ok=0 identities=2 selected_id=1 binders_consumed=0 binders_remain=0
[B_ids1_binders2_selected0] parse_ok=1 identities=1 selected_id=0 binders_consumed=1 binders_remain=33
[B_ids1_binders2_selected1] parse_ok=1 identities=1 selected_id=1 binders_consumed=2 binders_remain=0
```

Interpretation:

1. If the selected identity requires a binder index that does not exist, parsing fails.
2. If extra binders exist and the selected identity is earlier, parsing can succeed and leave trailing binder bytes.
3. This matches "validate selected binder", but does not enforce a unified "global one-to-one count equality" hard failure.

## 5. Root Cause of Inconsistency

The inconsistency comes from enforcement granularity:

1. RFC semantics describe full correspondence (`one for each`, same order).
2. OpenSSL server path focuses on selected-identity binder validation.
3. This path enforces selected binder availability and length, but does not always reject global count mismatch as a separate hard check.

labels.description: The issue is not considered a bug
labels.name: resolved: not a bug

items.title: OfferedPsks.identities lower-bound inconsistency

items.body:
# PskIdentity.identity lower-bound inconsistency

## 1. Conclusion

This issue concerns the minimum length constraint on `PskIdentity.identity`.
RFC 8446 explicitly requires `identity<1..2^16-1>`, but the current OpenSSL server parsing path does not uniformly reject `identity_len = 0`. Therefore this item is classified as **Partially Satisfied**.

## 2. RFC 8446 English Source Text

### 2.1 `pre_shared_key` structure and `identity` constraint

From [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 4.2.11](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.2.11):

```text
The "extension_data" field of this extension contains a
"PreSharedKeyExtension" value:

   struct {
       opaque identity<1..2^16-1>;
       uint32 obfuscated_ticket_age;
   } PskIdentity;

   opaque PskBinderEntry<32..255>;

   struct {
       PskIdentity identities<7..2^16-1>;
       PskBinderEntry binders<33..2^16-1>;
   } OfferedPsks;
```

The key line is:

```text
opaque identity<1..2^16-1>;
```

### 2.2 `identity` field semantic description

From [RFC 8446, Section 4.2.11](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.2.11):

```text
identity:  A label for a key.  For instance, a ticket (as defined in
   Appendix B.3.4) or a label for a pre-shared key established
   externally.
```

### 2.3 Vector syntax lower bound and parse-failure semantics

From [RFC 8446, Section 3.4](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-3.4):

```text
Variable-length vectors are defined by specifying a subrange of legal
lengths, inclusively, using the notation <floor..ceiling>.
```

From [RFC 8446, Section 6](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-6):

```text
Peers which receive a message which cannot be parsed according to the
syntax ... or contain an out-of-range length) MUST terminate the
connection with a "decode_error" alert.
```

## 3. OpenSSL Code Description

### 3.1 How `identity` is parsed

In `tls_parse_ctos_psk()` in `ssl/statem/extensions_srvr.c`, each identity is processed as:

```c
if (!PACKET_get_length_prefixed_2(&identities, &identity)
    || !PACKET_get_net_4(&identities, &ticket_agel)) {
    SSLfatal(...);
    return 0;
}

idlen = PACKET_remaining(&identity);
```

`idlen` is read here, but there is no unified hard-fail check enforcing `idlen >= 1`.

### 3.2 Subsequent processing does not treat an empty identity as a syntax-layer error

Later code enters callback, lookup, or ticket-flow branches, for example:

```c
if (sess == NULL
    && s->psk_server_callback != NULL
    && idlen <= PSK_MAX_IDENTITY_LEN) {
    ...
}
```

This only checks an upper bound; it is not equivalent to the RFC lower-bound hard check.

## 4. Root Cause of Inconsistency

The core mismatch is:

1. RFC defines `identity<1..>` as a syntactic validity constraint (identity must be non-empty).
2. The OpenSSL path primarily checks parseability, bounds safety, and whether processing can continue semantically. It does not enforce a minimum-length hard failure at the identity read point.
3. As a result, a syntactically invalid empty identity may continue into subsequent branches instead of being rejected immediately.

## 5. Test Evidence

Runtime output from `runtime_retest/id307/test_psk_identities_lower_bound.out`:

```text
[single_zero_len_identity_len6] parse_ok=1 entries=1 zero_len_identity_seen=1
```

`identity_len = 0` still passes through this parsing segment, which supports the conclusion that no unified hard failure exists.
items.title: PskIdentity.identity lower-bound inconsistency

items.body:
# TLSPlaintext.legacy_record_version inconsistency 

## 1. Conclusion

Looking at the `TLSPlaintext.legacy_record_version` requirement alone, OpenSSL is closer to **Not Satisfied** than to "Fully Satisfied".

The reason is:

- RFC 8446 requires that `TLSPlaintext.legacy_record_version` MUST be ignored.
- OpenSSL does have an "ignore this field" implementation note on the path where TLS 1.3 plaintext has already been confirmed.
- But on the earlier `TLS_ANY_VERSION` path, OpenSSL still reads and checks whether the high byte of `rec->rec_version` belongs to the `0x03xx` family, so the field is not ignored in all scenarios.

Therefore this sub-issue is more accurately described as: the handling of `TLSPlaintext.legacy_record_version` is not fully consistent with RFC 8446.

## 2. RFC 8446 English Source Text

### 2.1 TLSPlaintext structure definition requirement

From [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 5.1](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-5.1):

```text
legacy_record_version:  MUST be set to 0x0303 for all records
   generated by a TLS 1.3 implementation other than an initial
   ClientHello (i.e., one not generated after a HelloRetryRequest),
   where it MAY also be 0x0301 for compatibility purposes.  This
   field is deprecated and MUST be ignored for all purposes.
   Previous versions of TLS would use other values in this field
   under some circumstances.
```

The key sentence is:

```text
This field is deprecated and MUST be ignored for all purposes.
```

### 2.2 Appendix D summary of this field

From [RFC 8446, Appendix D](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#appendix-D):

```text
(TLSPlaintext.legacy_record_version and
TLSCiphertext.legacy_record_version) for various purposes.  As of
TLS 1.3, this field is deprecated.  The value of
TLSPlaintext.legacy_record_version MUST be ignored by all
implementations.  The value of TLSCiphertext.legacy_record_version is
included in the additional data for deprotection but MAY otherwise be
ignored or MAY be validated to match the fixed constant value.
Version negotiation is performed using only the handshake versions
```

The most critical normative sentence here is:

```text
TLSPlaintext.legacy_record_version MUST be ignored by all implementations.
```

And the spec also states:

```text
Version negotiation is performed using only the handshake versions
```

That is, version negotiation must rely on handshake-layer fields, not on `TLSPlaintext.legacy_record_version`.

### 2.3 Receiver compatibility note

From [RFC 8446, Appendix D](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#appendix-D):

```text
Note that earlier versions of TLS did not clearly specify the record
layer version number value in all cases
(TLSPlaintext.legacy_record_version).  Servers will receive various
TLS 1.x versions in this field, but its value MUST always be ignored.
```

This again emphasizes:

```text
its value MUST always be ignored.
```

## 3. OpenSSL Code Description

### 3.1 OpenSSL behavior when the TLS version is not yet determined

From `ssl/record/methods/tlsany_meth.c`:

```c
static int tls_validate_record_header(OSSL_RECORD_LAYER *rl, TLS_RL_RECORD *rec)
{
    if (rl->version == TLS_ANY_VERSION) {
        if ((rec->rec_version >> 8) != SSL3_VERSION_MAJOR) {
            if (rl->is_first_record) {
                unsigned char *p;

                /*
                 * Go back to start of packet, look at the five bytes that
                 * we have.
                 */
                p = rl->packet;
                if (HAS_PREFIX((char *)p, "GET ") || HAS_PREFIX((char *)p, "POST ") || HAS_PREFIX((char *)p, "HEAD ") || HAS_PREFIX((char *)p, "PATCH") || HAS_PREFIX((char *)p, "OPTIO") || HAS_PREFIX((char *)p, "DELET") || HAS_PREFIX((char *)p, "TRACE") || HAS_PREFIX((char *)p, "PUT ")) {
                    RLAYERfatal(rl, SSL_AD_NO_ALERT, SSL_R_HTTP_REQUEST);
                    return 0;
                } else if (HAS_PREFIX((char *)p, "CONNE")) {
                    RLAYERfatal(rl, SSL_AD_NO_ALERT,
                        SSL_R_HTTPS_PROXY_REQUEST);
                    return 0;
                }

                /* Doesn't look like TLS - don't send an alert */
                RLAYERfatal(rl, SSL_AD_NO_ALERT,
                    SSL_R_WRONG_VERSION_NUMBER);
                return 0;
            } else {
                RLAYERfatal(rl, SSL_AD_PROTOCOL_VERSION,
                    SSL_R_WRONG_VERSION_NUMBER);
                return 0;
            }
        }
```

This code shows:

- When `rl->version == TLS_ANY_VERSION`, OpenSSL has not yet determined the final protocol version.
- But it already inspects `rec->rec_version`.
- The specific check is:

```c
if ((rec->rec_version >> 8) != SSL3_VERSION_MAJOR)
```

This requires the high byte of the record header version to look like `0x03xx`.

So on this early path, `TLSPlaintext.legacy_record_version` is not fully ignored — it is used for initial filtering.

### 3.2 OpenSSL behavior when TLS 1.3 plaintext path is already known

```c
    } else if (rl->version == TLS1_3_VERSION) {
        /*
         * In this case we know we are going to negotiate TLSv1.3, but we've
         * had an HRR, so we haven't actually done so yet. In TLSv1.3 we
         * must ignore the legacy record version in plaintext records.
         */
```

This comment is direct: the OpenSSL developers know the RFC requirement and do follow "ignore the legacy record version in plaintext records" on this branch.

### 3.3 Version comparison in the non-TLS-1.3 branch

```c
    } else if (rec->rec_version != rl->version) {
        if ((rl->version & 0xFF00) == (rec->rec_version & 0xFF00)) {
            if (rec->type == SSL3_RT_ALERT) {
                RLAYERfatal(rl, SSL_AD_NO_ALERT,
                    SSL_R_WRONG_VERSION_NUMBER);
                return 0;
            }
            /* Send back error using their minor version number */
            rl->version = (unsigned short)rec->rec_version;
        }
        RLAYERfatal(rl, SSL_AD_PROTOCOL_VERSION,
            SSL_R_WRONG_VERSION_NUMBER);
        return 0;
    }
```

This further shows that OpenSSL's record layer treats `rec->rec_version` as a field that participates in decisions in many cases, rather than unconditionally ignoring it.

## 4. Where the Inconsistency Lies

The core difference between the standard and the implementation is the scope of "ignore".

The RFC requirement is unconditional and global:

```text
TLSPlaintext.legacy_record_version MUST be ignored by all implementations.
```

And also:

```text
its value MUST always be ignored.
```

This means:

- The field must not be used to filter input even when the version has not yet been negotiated.
- It must not serve as an early indicator of "looks like TLS".
- Version negotiation must rely only on handshake-layer version fields.

But OpenSSL's implementation is conditional:

- When `rl->version == TLS1_3_VERSION`: the field is ignored.
- When `rl->version == TLS_ANY_VERSION`: the high byte of `rec->rec_version` is checked against `0x03`.

So what OpenSSL actually does is:

- "Ignore this field after the TLS 1.3 plaintext context has been confirmed."

While RFC requires:

- "Always ignore `TLSPlaintext.legacy_record_version` itself."

These two are not the same semantic strength.

## 5. Why This Inconsistency Exists

This inconsistency looks more like a gap between "implementation-layer early filtering strategy" and "RFC ideal semantics".

Likely implementation motivations include:

- Quickly distinguishing "TLS-like input" from "clearly non-TLS input" at the earliest record header read.
- Early interception of HTTP requests, proxy requests, or obviously malformed first packets.
- Reusing existing record-layer version filtering logic rather than pushing the TLS 1.3 "ignore legacy_record_version" semantics all the way back to the very first path.

Regardless of implementation motivation, from the literal text of RFC 8446, this approach is stricter than the spec requires and uses `TLSPlaintext.legacy_record_version` earlier than the spec allows, which creates the inconsistency.

## 6. Final Assessment

Evaluating only the `TLSPlaintext.legacy_record_version` sub-issue:

- Standard requirement: MUST be ignored
- OpenSSL implementation: ignored only after the TLS 1.3 plaintext path is confirmed; on the earlier path the field is still read and used for filtering
- Conclusion: not fully consistent

In the broader context of this item, the reason it was previously described as "partially satisfied" is:

- The `TLSPlaintext` half leans toward "not satisfied"
- The `TLSCiphertext` half permits implementations to validate the fixed constant value `0x0303`

labels.name: issue: bug report

items.body:
# HRR Group Check Failure Uses `handshake_failure` Instead of `illegal_parameter`

## 1. Summary

The core issue is not that mbedTLS skips the HRR group consistency check. Rather:

- mbedTLS **does** compare the group selected in `ServerHello` against the group indicated by `HelloRetryRequest` and aborts when they differ.
- But the alert queued on mismatch is `handshake_failure(40)` instead of the TLS 1.3-mandated `illegal_parameter(47)`.

Both alert constants are defined in the codebase. The inconsistency is purely in the alert-code selection for this specific condition.

Therefore, this item reflects a standards-relevant alert-mapping mismatch: the core verification logic is present and the handshake is aborted correctly, but the alert mapping does not match the RFC requirement.

## 2. Specification Description

### 2.1 Relevant TLS 1.3 requirement

From [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 4.2.8](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.2.8):

```text
   If using (EC)DHE key establishment and a HelloRetryRequest containing a
   "key_share" extension was received by the client, the client MUST
   verify that the selected NamedGroup in the ServerHello is the same as
   that in the HelloRetryRequest.  If this check fails, the client MUST
   abort the handshake with an "illegal_parameter" alert.
```

The key requirement:

- after receiving a `HelloRetryRequest` with a `key_share` extension
- the client must compare the selected group in `ServerHello` with the one requested by `HelloRetryRequest`
- if they differ, the client must abort with `illegal_parameter`

This is a `MUST` requirement.

## 3. Code Description

### 3.1 Client-side HRR follow-up group check

From `library/ssl_tls13_client.c`:

```c
/* Check that the chosen group matches the one we offered. */
offered_group = ssl->handshake->offered_group_id;
if (offered_group != group) {
    MBEDTLS_SSL_DEBUG_MSG(
        1, ("Invalid server key share, our group %u, their group %u",
            (unsigned) offered_group, (unsigned) group));
    MBEDTLS_SSL_PEND_FATAL_ALERT(MBEDTLS_SSL_ALERT_MSG_HANDSHAKE_FAILURE,
                                 MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_HANDSHAKE_FAILURE);
    return MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_HANDSHAKE_FAILURE;
}
```

This branch shows that mbedTLS does perform the semantic check: it compares the server-selected group with `ssl->handshake->offered_group_id` and aborts when the two groups differ.

### 3.2 The alert that is actually queued is `handshake_failure`

From `include/mbedtls/ssl.h`:

```c
#define MBEDTLS_SSL_ALERT_MSG_HANDSHAKE_FAILURE     40  /* 0x28 */
```

```c
#define MBEDTLS_SSL_ALERT_MSG_ILLEGAL_PARAMETER     47  /* 0x2F */
```

The code above queues alert `40` (`handshake_failure`) rather than alert `47` (`illegal_parameter`).

### 3.3 The queued alert is sent as-is

From `library/ssl_msg.c`:

```c
ret = mbedtls_ssl_send_alert_message(ssl,
                                     MBEDTLS_SSL_ALERT_LEVEL_FATAL,
                                     ssl->alert_type);
```

The pending-alert mechanism sends `ssl->alert_type` directly. The alert is not re-mapped later to `illegal_parameter`.

## 4. Inconsistency

The inconsistency is narrow but real:

- the RFC requires the client to abort with `illegal_parameter`
- mbedTLS aborts with `handshake_failure`

The problem is not that the check is missing. The problem is that the condition is detected correctly, but the resulting alert constant does not match the RFC-mandated alert.

## 5. Why This Is Only Partially Satisfied

This item is classified as `Partially Satisfied` rather than a total failure because mbedTLS still enforces the core safety property:

- it detects that the `ServerHello` group is inconsistent with the HRR-selected group
- it aborts the handshake immediately

However, the RFC is stricter than "abort somehow":

- it requires a specific alert: `illegal_parameter`
- mbedTLS uses `handshake_failure` instead

So the behavioral check is present, but the alert mapping is not RFC-accurate.

## 6. Conclusion

TLS 1.3 requires that if the group selected in `ServerHello` does not match the group indicated by `HelloRetryRequest`, the client must abort with an `illegal_parameter` alert.

mbedTLS does implement the comparison and does abort on mismatch, which means the core verification logic exists. But the implementation queues and sends `handshake_failure` rather than `illegal_parameter`.

labels.description: The issue was opened to report a bug
labels.name: issue: bug report

items.title: HRR Group Check Failure Uses `handshake_failure` Instead of `illegal_parameter`

```c
static SSL *alloc_port_user_ssl(QUIC_CHANNEL *ch, void *arg)
{
    QUIC_LISTENER *ql = arg;
    QUIC_CONNECTION *qc = create_qc_from_incoming_conn(ql, ch);

    return (qc == NULL) ? NULL : &qc->obj.ssl;
}
```
`create_qc_from_incoming_conn()` calls `qc = OPENSSL_zalloc(sizeof(*qc))) == NULL`, and returns a reference to `->obj.ssl`. When `qc` goes out of scope, memleak is created.

items.body:
It is unclear what the error return values for the `i2d_*` functions are. Docs point to negative return values on error, but the implementation implies 0 can also be returned on error.

For example, [the manpage for `i2d_DHparams`](https://docs.openssl.org/master/man3/d2i_RSAPrivateKey/#return-values) or [the manpage for `i2d_ASN1_TYPE`](https://docs.openssl.org/master/man3/d2i_X509/#return-values) states that a negative value is returned on error. (Similarly in general for `i2d_TYPE` functions).

Back in OpenSSL 1.1.1, [the docs](https://docs.openssl.org/1.1.1/man3/d2i_DHparams/#return-values) explicitly mentioned that 0 could also be returned on error. But that documentation was removed in https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/13138 and started referring to other doc pages.

Worth pointing out too: LibreSSL, which inherited from OpenSSL, documents some functions as returning `<= 0` on error and some not (e.g. https://man.openbsd.org/d2i_DHparams.3 states `a value <= 0 if an error occurs`).

When looking at the current OpenSSL implementation, all these `i2d_ASN1_TYPE` functions follow a call chain: `i2d_ASN1_TYPE -> ASN1_item_i2d -> asn1_item_flags_i2d -> ASN1_item_ex_i2d`. Notably `asn1_item_flags_i2d` checks for a `<= 0` error value here: https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/8fa8acf0362f55e539bf61a86ddbdcbb57eeb2b6/crypto/asn1/tasn_enc.c#L62-L64
Looking at the code from `ASN1_item_ex_i2d`, I see multiple conditions leading to an error value of 0:
* https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/8fa8acf0362f55e539bf61a86ddbdcbb57eeb2b6/crypto/asn1/tasn_enc.c#L90-L92
* https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/8fa8acf0362f55e539bf61a86ddbdcbb57eeb2b6/crypto/asn1/tasn_enc.c#L136-L138
* https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/8fa8acf0362f55e539bf61a86ddbdcbb57eeb2b6/crypto/asn1/tasn_enc.c#L154-L156
* https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/8fa8acf0362f55e539bf61a86ddbdcbb57eeb2b6/crypto/asn1/tasn_enc.c#L169-L170
* https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/8fa8acf0362f55e539bf61a86ddbdcbb57eeb2b6/crypto/asn1/tasn_enc.c#L176-L178
* https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/8fa8acf0362f55e539bf61a86ddbdcbb57eeb2b6/crypto/asn1/tasn_enc.c#L203-L204
* Probably some other transitive calls as well


Admittedly I'm not super familiar with this stuff. But to me it seems that the documentation is incorrect. Could you please clarify this? Thanks in advance!

Disclaimer: This was found by a hybrid static-dynamic analyser that looks for inconsistent handling of error checks in bindings.

items.body:
Running ```openssl ca``` to sign a certificate fails with the wrong error message if the serial file
is corrupted. This happens both in openssl 1.1.1w and openssl 3.5.6.  One of our customers hit this bug this week and it took hours to track down.

Steps to reproduce:

1) create a CA directory structure and a self signed CA cert. 
2) corrupt the file 'serial" editing the file and setting the contents to the number of a cert already issued.
3) create a new signing request and attempt to sign it

You will see an error like this:

```
openssl ca -batch \
-config signhost.conf \
-name hostca -in /tmp/certs/stones/norm/hosts/moop/moop.csr.pem \
-out /tmp/certs/stones/norm/hosts/moop/moop.cert.pem -notext \
-cert /tmp/certs/stones/norm/stoneCA/hostCA-norm.cert.pem \
-keyfile /tmp/certs/stones/norm/stoneCA/hostCA-norm.privkey.pem -days 30

Using configuration from signhost.conf
ca: Error on line 8 of config file "signhost.conf"
40370B00547F0000:error:07000068:configuration file routines:str_copy:variable has no value:../../externals/openssl/crypto/conf/conf_def.c:758:line 8
```

which complains about line 8 in the config file that uses ${ENV}:

```
dir = ${ENV::stoneCaDir}
```

But that is not the real error! The env var is correctly set.
The real error is the bad state of the file 'serial', which contains the number
of an already issued certificate.

Proof: edit the conf file at line 8 (and any other references to ${ENV}) to replace all
 ${ENV:xxx} entries with the real values of the env vars. Then run the command again.
NOW you will see the correct error message that serial number 1000 has already been issued:

```
openssl ca -batch -config signhost.conf \
 -name hostca -in /tmp/certs/stones/norm/hosts/moop/moop.csr.pem \
-out /tmp/certs/stones/norm/hosts/moop/moop.cert.pem -notext \
-cert /tmp/certs/stones/norm/stoneCA/hostCA-norm.cert.pem \
-keyfile /tmp/certs/stones/norm/stoneCA/hostCA-norm.privkey.pem -days 30

Using configuration from signhost.conf
Check that the request matches the signature
Signature ok
The Subject's Distinguished Name is as follows
gemstone_CertificateType:ASN.1 12:'host'
gemstone_SubnetRestriction:ASN.1 12:'0.0.0.0/0'
gemstone_StoneName    :ASN.1 12:'norm'
commonName            :ASN.1 12:'moop'
ERROR:Serial number 1000 has already been issued,
      check the database/serial_file for corruption
The matching entry has the following details
Type          :Valid
Expires on    :260529233037Z
Serial Number :1000
File name     :unknown
Subject Name  :/gsStoneName=norm/gsCertType=hostCA/CN=norm_hostCA
```

items.body: OpenSSL 3.6 vendoring, OQS Falcon-only 빌드, HYBCERT_* 매크로 SSOT 확립. 완료 기준 5항목 달성.
items.title: Phase 1: Base bump + repo bootstrap

items.body:
## Problem

After Phase 2+3 merge, `gen-catalyst` and `gen-chameleon` failed with ML-DSA-65 keys:

1. **OID lookup failure**: `EVP_PKEY_get_id(ml_dsa_key)` returns `EVP_PKEY_KEYMGMT` for provider-based keys, causing `OBJ_nid2obj` to return NULL.
2. **Extensions not persisted**: `X509_add_ext()` does NOT set `cert_info.enc.modified`, so `PEM_write_bio_X509` emits stale cached DER — extensions added after cert load are silently dropped.

## Fix

- Add `pkey_sig_nid()` helper: falls back to `EVP_PKEY_get0_type_name()` + `OBJ_ln2nid()` for provider keys
- `write_x509_pem()`: call `i2d_re_X509_tbs()` before write to force TBS re-encoding

## Verification

```
hybcert gen-catalyst -alt-key ml-dsa-65.key -cert base.cert -out catalyst.cert  ✓
hybcert gen-chameleon -base-cert base.cert -pq-key ml-dsa-65.key -out cham.cert  ✓
hybcert gen-related -classic base.cert -pq pq.cert                               ✓
hybcert_x509_test: 6/6 PASS
```

items.body:
The ICC intel compiler is deprecated and replaced by the ICX (clang based) compiler.
Some users/companies allow critical CVS's fixes to be included into the Fips module.
Building the Fips library is done by using the latests of the OpenSSL branch version in which the Fips module was verified. 
OpenSSL 3.5 => Fips 140-3
OpenSSL 3.0 => Fips 140-2
Without PR 30313 being merged into 3.0 and 3.5, the Fips modules would face a significant performance drop for users they have to change to the ICX compiler (due to not using optimised ASM).

This issue is requesting the PR 30313 to be merged into OpenSSL 3.0 and OpenSSL 3.5 branch.
items.title: Request PR 30313 (Add icx compiler version support in perl asm scripts) to be merged in branches they are used for Fips validation

items.body:
### Summary

For SLH-DSA raw private keys imported with `EVP_PKEY_new_raw_private_key_ex()`, OpenSSL accepts exact-length component-mutated private keys.

For these mutated keys, `EVP_PKEY_check()` and `EVP_PKEY_pairwise_check()` reject the key, but `EVP_DigestSign()` still succeeds and produces a signature that does not verify under the public key exported from the same `EVP_PKEY` object.

This report is not claiming that `EVP_PKEY_check()` or `EVP_PKEY_pairwise_check()` are broken. The behavior I would like to clarify is whether signing should proceed after these consistency checks would reject the key.

### Minimal reproducer

A minimal standalone reproducer is attached:

- `openssl_slh_dsa_raw_private_key_consistency_repro.cpp`

The reproducer:

1. Generates an `SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s` key.
2. Exports the raw private key with `EVP_PKEY_get_raw_private_key()`.
3. Mutates one byte in selected raw private-key components while preserving the exact raw private-key length.
4. Re-imports the mutated raw private key with `EVP_PKEY_new_raw_private_key_ex()`.
5. Runs `EVP_PKEY_public_check()`, `EVP_PKEY_private_check()`, `EVP_PKEY_check()`, and `EVP_PKEY_pairwise_check()`.
6. Calls `EVP_DigestSign()`.
7. Verifies the resulting signature using the public key exported from the same imported `EVP_PKEY` object.

### Observed behavior

```text
OpenSSL version: OpenSSL 3.5.6 7 Apr 2026

algorithm,mutation,raw_private_len,import,public_check,private_check,key_check,pairwise_check,sign,self_verify,exported_public_verify,public_export,public_import,sig_len
SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s,valid,64,accept,1,1,1,1,success,1,1,yes,yes,7856
SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s,mutate_SK_seed,64,accept,1,1,0,0,success,0,0,yes,yes,7856
SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s,mutate_PK_seed,64,accept,1,1,0,0,success,0,0,yes,yes,7856
SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s,mutate_PK_root,64,accept,1,1,0,0,success,0,0,yes,yes,7856

[RESULT] signing succeeded with mutated raw private keys that failed key_check/pairwise_check; the signatures did not verify
exit_code=1
```

The valid control behaves as expected.

For the mutated `SK.seed`, `PK.seed`, and `PK.root` cases:

- raw private-key import succeeds;
- `EVP_PKEY_public_check()` and `EVP_PKEY_private_check()` return `1`;
- `EVP_PKEY_check()` and `EVP_PKEY_pairwise_check()` return `0`;
- `EVP_DigestSign()` succeeds;
- the produced signature fails verification with the same imported key object;
- the produced signature also fails verification with the public key exported from that same key object.

### Expected behavior / clarification request

Since `EVP_PKEY_check()` and `EVP_PKEY_pairwise_check()` reject these imported SLH-DSA keys, I would expect either:

1. `EVP_DigestSign()` to reject such keys before producing a signature; or
2. the documentation to clarify that callers must explicitly run `EVP_PKEY_check()` or `EVP_PKEY_pairwise_check()` after raw private key import, because signing may otherwise succeed with an inconsistent key and produce an unverifiable signature.

Could you clarify whether the current behavior is intentional?

### Environment

```text
OpenSSL 3.5.6 7 Apr 2026
Algorithm: SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s
Compiler: g++ 11.4.0 with -std=c++17
Platform: linux-x86_64
```

### Attachments

- `openssl_slh_dsa_raw_private_key_consistency_repro.cpp`
- `run_output_openssl_3_5_6.txt`
- `env_openssl_3_5_6_sanitized.txt`

[env_openssl_3_5_6_sanitized.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/27271509/env_openssl_3_5_6_sanitized.txt)
[openssl_slh_dsa_raw_private_key_consistency_repro.cpp](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/27271510/openssl_slh_dsa_raw_private_key_consistency_repro.cpp)
[run_output_openssl_3_5_6.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/27271511/run_output_openssl_3_5_6.txt)

items.body:
### Summary

This is related to the same general class of empty-input API expression issues as the KDF and EVP_MAC HMAC `NULL,0` cases, but I am filing it separately because this case exercises a different API path and different parameter semantics.

Here the affected input is the ML-DSA context string, not a KDF salt/key/secret or a MAC key. If maintainers prefer to track all `NULL,0` vs non-NULL,0 cases under one issue, I am happy to move the details there.

For ML-DSA, OpenSSL rejects an explicit empty context string when it is represented as `NULL,0`, but accepts the same empty context when it is represented as a non-NULL pointer with length `0`.

This affects both signing and verification initialization:

- `EVP_DigestSignInit_ex()` fails when the context string is `NULL,0`.
- `EVP_DigestVerifyInit_ex()` fails when the context string is `NULL,0`.
- A non-NULL pointer with length `0` is accepted.
- Omitting the context string is also accepted.

The behavior is reproduced for:

- `ML-DSA-44`
- `ML-DSA-65`
- `ML-DSA-87`

### Minimal reproducer

A minimal standalone reproducer is attached:

- `openssl_mldsa_context_null0_repro.cpp`

The reproducer generates an ML-DSA key for each parameter set and tests the following context-string cases:

- `omitted`: no context-string parameter is provided
- `NULL_0`: context-string pointer is `NULL`, length is `0`
- `dummy_0`: context-string pointer is non-NULL, length is `0`
- `nonempty_len3`: context string has length 3

For each algorithm, the reproducer checks signing. If `dummy_0` signing succeeds, it also verifies the resulting signature using:

- omitted context
- `NULL,0` context
- non-NULL,0 context

All other inputs are fixed.

### Observed behavior

Tested with OpenSSL 3.5.6.

```text
openssl_version,OpenSSL 3.5.6 7 Apr 2026
operation,algorithm,context_case,init_ok,success,verify_rc,sig_len,error_stage,error_text,sig_prefix8
sign,ML-DSA-44,omitted,1,1,1,2420,none,none,255ed2232e7635e9
sign,ML-DSA-44,NULL_0,0,0,-1,0,DigestSignInit,error:078C0102:common libcrypto routines::passed a null parameter,-
sign,ML-DSA-44,dummy_0,1,1,1,2420,none,none,6ba7285e8dfe8337
sign,ML-DSA-44,nonempty_len3,1,1,1,2420,none,none,9195c7912a318693
verify_dummy0_sig,ML-DSA-44,omitted,1,1,1,2420,none,none,-
verify_dummy0_sig,ML-DSA-44,NULL_0,0,0,-1,2420,DigestVerifyInit,error:078C0102:common libcrypto routines::passed a null parameter,-
verify_dummy0_sig,ML-DSA-44,dummy_0,1,1,1,2420,none,none,-
pair,ML-DSA-44,verify_context,null0_fails_dummy0_succeeds
pair,ML-DSA-44,sign_context,null0_fails_dummy0_succeeds
sign,ML-DSA-65,omitted,1,1,1,3309,none,none,abc4dcadf8746cfe
sign,ML-DSA-65,NULL_0,0,0,-1,0,DigestSignInit,error:078C0102:common libcrypto routines::passed a null parameter,-
sign,ML-DSA-65,dummy_0,1,1,1,3309,none,none,0f7bb1045e7f28b5
sign,ML-DSA-65,nonempty_len3,1,1,1,3309,none,none,1ffab0631d598e9a
verify_dummy0_sig,ML-DSA-65,omitted,1,1,1,3309,none,none,-
verify_dummy0_sig,ML-DSA-65,NULL_0,0,0,-1,3309,DigestVerifyInit,error:078C0102:common libcrypto routines::passed a null parameter,-
verify_dummy0_sig,ML-DSA-65,dummy_0,1,1,1,3309,none,none,-
pair,ML-DSA-65,verify_context,null0_fails_dummy0_succeeds
pair,ML-DSA-65,sign_context,null0_fails_dummy0_succeeds
sign,ML-DSA-87,omitted,1,1,1,4627,none,none,63ae19fd402e7665
sign,ML-DSA-87,NULL_0,0,0,-1,0,DigestSignInit,error:078C0102:common libcrypto routines::passed a null parameter,-
sign,ML-DSA-87,dummy_0,1,1,1,4627,none,none,edc8c12ba9a5e949
sign,ML-DSA-87,nonempty_len3,1,1,1,4627,none,none,1603ed744114303f
verify_dummy0_sig,ML-DSA-87,omitted,1,1,1,4627,none,none,-
verify_dummy0_sig,ML-DSA-87,NULL_0,0,0,-1,4627,DigestVerifyInit,error:078C0102:common libcrypto routines::passed a null parameter,-
verify_dummy0_sig,ML-DSA-87,dummy_0,1,1,1,4627,none,none,-
pair,ML-DSA-87,verify_context,null0_fails_dummy0_succeeds
pair,ML-DSA-87,sign_context,null0_fails_dummy0_succeeds
result,inconsistency_detected
exit_code=1
```

### Result summary

For all tested ML-DSA parameter sets:

```text
ML-DSA-44 sign context:
  NULL,0 fails
  non-NULL,0 succeeds

ML-DSA-44 verify context:
  NULL,0 fails
  non-NULL,0 succeeds

ML-DSA-65 sign context:
  NULL,0 fails
  non-NULL,0 succeeds

ML-DSA-65 verify context:
  NULL,0 fails
  non-NULL,0 succeeds

ML-DSA-87 sign context:
  NULL,0 fails
  non-NULL,0 succeeds

ML-DSA-87 verify context:
  NULL,0 fails
  non-NULL,0 succeeds
```

The failure occurs during initialization:

```text
DigestSignInit,error:078C0102:common libcrypto routines::passed a null parameter
DigestVerifyInit,error:078C0102:common libcrypto routines::passed a null parameter
```

The non-NULL,0 context is accepted and works as an empty context. Signatures produced with `dummy_0` verify successfully with `dummy_0`, and also verify successfully when the context string is omitted.

### Expected behavior

The ML-DSA context string should handle empty context representations consistently.

Since OpenSSL accepts a non-NULL pointer with length `0` as an empty context string, `NULL,0` should not fail solely because the pointer is `NULL`.

Expected behavior would be one of the following:

1. both `NULL,0` and non-NULL,0 are accepted as empty context strings; or
2. if `NULL,0` is intentionally rejected, the behavior is documented and the failure clearly indicates that this representation is unsupported.

The current behavior accepts one representation of an empty context string and rejects the other.

[env_openssl_3_5_6_sanitized.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/27312020/env_openssl_3_5_6_sanitized.txt)
[openssl_mldsa_context_null0_repro.cpp](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/27312019/openssl_mldsa_context_null0_repro.cpp)

items.title: ML-DSA signing and verification handle NULL,0 and non-NULL,0 inconsistently for an empty context string

items.body:
### Summary
This is related to the same general class of empty-input API expression issues as the EVP_MAC HMAC and ML-DSA context-string `NULL,0` cases, but I am filing it separately because this case exercises the KDF API paths and KDF parameter semantics.

Here the affected inputs are KDF octet-string parameters such as HKDF `salt`/`key` and TLS1-PRF `secret`, not a MAC key or a signature context string. If maintainers prefer to track all `NULL,0` vs non-NULL,0 cases under one issue, I am happy to move the details there.

OpenSSL KDF APIs treat two representations of a zero-length input buffer inconsistently:

```c
param = NULL;
param_len = 0;
```

and:

```c
unsigned char dummy = 0;
param = &dummy;
param_len = 0;
```

In the tested KDF paths, `NULL,0` is rejected with `passed a null parameter`, while a non-NULL pointer with length `0` is accepted and derives output.

This behavior was observed for both `EVP_KDF` and legacy `EVP_PKEY` KDF APIs:

- `EVP_KDF` HKDF `salt`
- `EVP_KDF` HKDF `key`
- legacy `EVP_PKEY` HKDF `salt`
- legacy `EVP_PKEY` HKDF `key`
- `EVP_KDF` TLS1-PRF `secret`
- legacy `EVP_PKEY` TLS1-PRF `secret`

The issue is not about different input contents. In all compared cases, the input length is zero. The only difference is whether the pointer is `NULL` or a valid dummy address.

### Minimal reproducer

A minimal standalone C++ reproducer is attached:

- `openssl_kdf_null0_repro.cpp`

The reproducer tests HKDF and TLS1-PRF through both APIs:

1. `EVP_KDF`
2. legacy `EVP_PKEY`

For each tested parameter, it compares:

- `NULL_0`: pointer is `NULL`, length is `0`
- `dummy_0`: pointer is non-NULL, length is `0`

All other parameters are fixed.

For `EVP_KDF`, parameters are passed directly to `EVP_KDF_derive()`, so `set_param_ok` is shown as `-1`.



### Observed behavior

Tested with OpenSSL 3.5.6.

```text
openssl_version,OpenSSL 3.5.6 7 Apr 2026
api,algorithm,param,case,set_param_ok,derive_ok,error_stage,error_text,out_prefix8
EVP_KDF,HKDF,salt,NULL_0,-1,0,derive,error:078C0102:common libcrypto routines::passed a null parameter,-
EVP_KDF,HKDF,salt,dummy_0,-1,1,none,none,bcea043d917acb3f
EVP_KDF,HKDF,key,NULL_0,-1,0,derive,error:078C0102:common libcrypto routines::passed a null parameter,-
EVP_KDF,HKDF,key,dummy_0,-1,1,none,none,59ea20fb2ec911b8
legacy_EVP_PKEY,HKDF,salt,NULL_0,0,0,set_param,error:078C0102:common libcrypto routines::passed a null parameter,-
legacy_EVP_PKEY,HKDF,salt,dummy_0,1,1,none,none,bcea043d917acb3f
legacy_EVP_PKEY,HKDF,key,NULL_0,0,0,set_param,error:078C0102:common libcrypto routines::passed a null parameter,-
legacy_EVP_PKEY,HKDF,key,dummy_0,1,1,none,none,59ea20fb2ec911b8
EVP_KDF,TLS1-PRF,secret,NULL_0,-1,0,derive,error:078C0102:common libcrypto routines::passed a null parameter,-
EVP_KDF,TLS1-PRF,secret,dummy_0,-1,1,none,none,2bc6fa26d002c80e
legacy_EVP_PKEY,TLS1-PRF,secret,NULL_0,0,0,set_param,error:078C0102:common libcrypto routines::passed a null parameter,-
legacy_EVP_PKEY,TLS1-PRF,secret,dummy_0,1,1,none,none,2bc6fa26d002c80e
pair,EVP_KDF,HKDF,salt,null0_fails_dummy0_succeeds
pair,EVP_KDF,HKDF,key,null0_fails_dummy0_succeeds
pair,legacy_EVP_PKEY,HKDF,salt,null0_fails_dummy0_succeeds
pair,legacy_EVP_PKEY,HKDF,key,null0_fails_dummy0_succeeds
pair,EVP_KDF,TLS1-PRF,secret,null0_fails_dummy0_succeeds
pair,legacy_EVP_PKEY,TLS1-PRF,secret,null0_fails_dummy0_succeeds
result,inconsistency_detected
exit_code=1
```

### Result summary

The same pattern appears in all tested KDF cases:

```text
EVP_KDF HKDF salt:                NULL,0 fails; dummy,0 succeeds
EVP_KDF HKDF key:                 NULL,0 fails; dummy,0 succeeds
legacy EVP_PKEY HKDF salt:        NULL,0 fails; dummy,0 succeeds
legacy EVP_PKEY HKDF key:         NULL,0 fails; dummy,0 succeeds
EVP_KDF TLS1-PRF secret:          NULL,0 fails; dummy,0 succeeds
legacy EVP_PKEY TLS1-PRF secret:  NULL,0 fails; dummy,0 succeeds
```

For `NULL,0`, the failure is:

```text
error:078C0102:common libcrypto routines::passed a null parameter
```

For `dummy,0`, the operation succeeds and derives output.

### Expected behavior

Zero-length input buffers should be handled consistently.

If a KDF parameter accepts `dummy,0` as a valid zero-length input, then `NULL,0` should not fail solely because the pointer is `NULL`.

Expected behavior would be one of the following:

1. both `NULL,0` and `dummy,0` are accepted for zero-length inputs; or
2. both representations are rejected consistently if zero-length input is not valid for that parameter.

The current behavior accepts one zero-length representation and rejects the other.

[env_openssl_3_5_6_sanitized.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/27312048/env_openssl_3_5_6_sanitized.txt)
[openssl_kdf_null0_repro.cpp](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/27312049/openssl_kdf_null0_repro.cpp)

items.title: EVP_KDF and legacy EVP_PKEY KDF APIs handle NULL,0 and non-NULL,0 inconsistently for zero-length inputs

items.body:
### Summary
This is related to the same general class of empty-input API expression issues as the KDF and ML-DSA context-string `NULL,0` cases, but I am filing it separately because this case exercises the `EVP_MAC` HMAC key-setting paths.

Here the affected input is the HMAC key, not a KDF salt/key/secret or a signature context string. If maintainers prefer to track all `NULL,0` vs non-NULL,0 cases under one issue, I am happy to move the details there.

`EVP_MAC` HMAC handles a zero-length key differently depending on the pointer value.

For HMAC-SHA256 with the same message and digest:

- `EVP_MAC` HMAC rejects `NULL,0` as the key.
- `EVP_MAC` HMAC accepts a non-NULL pointer with length `0`.
- When the non-NULL,0 key is accepted, the output matches classic `HMAC()`.
- classic `HMAC()` accepts both `NULL,0` and non-NULL,0 and produces the same output.

The same behavior appears in two `EVP_MAC` key-setting paths:

1. key passed directly to `EVP_MAC_init(ctx, key, key_len, params)`
2. key passed through `OSSL_MAC_PARAM_KEY`

The failing `EVP_MAC` `NULL,0` cases return failure from `EVP_MAC_init()`, and the error queue is empty.

### Minimal reproducer

A minimal standalone reproducer is attached:

- `openssl_evp_mac_hmac_null0_repro.cpp`

The reproducer computes HMAC-SHA256 over a fixed message using:

1. `EVP_MAC` HMAC with the key passed directly to `EVP_MAC_init()`
2. `EVP_MAC` HMAC with the key passed through `OSSL_MAC_PARAM_KEY`
3. classic `HMAC()` as a reference path

For each path, it tests:

- `NULL_0`: key pointer is `NULL`, key length is `0`
- `dummy_0`: key pointer is non-NULL, key length is `0`
- `nonempty`: normal non-empty key control case

All other inputs are fixed.

### Observed behavior

Tested with OpenSSL 3.5.6.

```text
openssl_version,OpenSSL 3.5.6 7 Apr 2026
api,algorithm,path,case,success,error_stage,error_text,out_prefix8
EVP_MAC,HMAC,EVP_MAC_init_key,NULL_0,0,init,none,-
EVP_MAC,HMAC,EVP_MAC_init_key,dummy_0,1,none,none,f67e86496a87a76a
EVP_MAC,HMAC,EVP_MAC_init_key,nonempty,1,none,none,7055c0f8249946ee
EVP_MAC,HMAC,OSSL_PARAM_KEY,NULL_0,0,init,none,-
EVP_MAC,HMAC,OSSL_PARAM_KEY,dummy_0,1,none,none,f67e86496a87a76a
EVP_MAC,HMAC,OSSL_PARAM_KEY,nonempty,1,none,none,7055c0f8249946ee
classic_HMAC,HMAC,HMAC_call,NULL_0,1,none,none,f67e86496a87a76a
classic_HMAC,HMAC,HMAC_call,dummy_0,1,none,none,f67e86496a87a76a
classic_HMAC,HMAC,HMAC_call,nonempty,1,none,none,7055c0f8249946ee
pair,EVP_MAC,HMAC,EVP_MAC_init_key,null0_fails_dummy0_succeeds
pair,EVP_MAC,HMAC,OSSL_PARAM_KEY,null0_fails_dummy0_succeeds
pair,classic_HMAC,HMAC,HMAC_call,both_succeed_same_output
compare,EVP_MAC_init_dummy0_vs_classic_dummy0,same_output
compare,EVP_MAC_param_dummy0_vs_classic_dummy0,same_output
compare,EVP_MAC_init_nonempty_vs_classic_nonempty,same_output
compare,EVP_MAC_param_nonempty_vs_classic_nonempty,same_output
result,inconsistency_detected
exit_code=1
```

### Result summary

The result shows the following behavior:

```text
EVP_MAC HMAC, key passed directly to EVP_MAC_init():
  NULL,0 fails
  non-NULL,0 succeeds

EVP_MAC HMAC, key passed through OSSL_MAC_PARAM_KEY:
  NULL,0 fails
  non-NULL,0 succeeds

classic HMAC():
  NULL,0 succeeds
  non-NULL,0 succeeds
  both produce the same output
```

When `EVP_MAC` accepts the key, its output matches classic `HMAC()`:

```text
compare,EVP_MAC_init_dummy0_vs_classic_dummy0,same_output
compare,EVP_MAC_param_dummy0_vs_classic_dummy0,same_output
compare,EVP_MAC_init_nonempty_vs_classic_nonempty,same_output
compare,EVP_MAC_param_nonempty_vs_classic_nonempty,same_output
```

For the failing `EVP_MAC` `NULL,0` cases, `EVP_MAC_init()` returns failure and no error text is reported:

```text
EVP_MAC,HMAC,EVP_MAC_init_key,NULL_0,0,init,none,-
EVP_MAC,HMAC,OSSL_PARAM_KEY,NULL_0,0,init,none,-
```

### Expected behavior

`EVP_MAC` HMAC should handle empty keys consistently.

Since `EVP_MAC` HMAC accepts a non-NULL pointer with length `0`, and that output matches classic `HMAC()`, `NULL,0` should not fail only because the key pointer is `NULL`.

Expected behavior would be one of the following:

1. `EVP_MAC` HMAC accepts both `NULL,0` and non-NULL,0 as empty keys; or
2. if `NULL,0` is intentionally rejected, the behavior is documented and the failure provides a clear error.

The current behavior accepts one representation of an empty key and rejects the other.

[openssl_evp_mac_hmac_null0_repro.cpp](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/27312064/openssl_evp_mac_hmac_null0_repro.cpp)

[env_openssl_3_5_6_sanitized.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/27312068/env_openssl_3_5_6_sanitized.txt)

labels.description: The issue was opened to report a bug
labels.name: issue: bug report

items.title: EVP_MAC HMAC handles NULL,0 and non-NULL,0 inconsistently for an empty key

items.body:
There is a top level include reading:

```c
#if defined(OPENSSL_SYS_UNIX) \
    && defined(OPENSSL_THREADS) && !defined(OPENSSL_NO_ASYNC) \
    && !defined(__ANDROID__) && !defined(__OpenBSD__) \
    && !defined(OPENSSL_SYS_TANDEM)
..
```

.. inside is an another include:

```c
..
#   if defined(OPENSSL_SYS_TANDEM)
#    include <tdmsig.h>
#   else
#    include <ucontext.h>
#   endif
..
```

which is dead code as the head ifdef is TANDEM not defined and inside we check if it's defined. This was introduced in patch https://github.com/openssl/openssl/commit/b2ed1b72fa56e71d53af213e6e5557dcce171a2c

items.body:
When building a certificate profile in openssl.cnf for a cross certificate, the policyMappings extension cannot process multiple mappings from one issuer policy to multiple subject policies.

In this case, the second occurrence of the issuer:subject mapping wins.

Example snippet from my profile:

```
policyMappings = @cross_cert_mappings

[ cross_cert_mappings ]
2.16.840.1.999.1 = 2.16.840.1.666.1
2.16.840.1.999.2 = 2.16.840.1.666.2
2.16.840.1.999.2 = 2.16.840.1.777.1
2.16.840.1.999.3 = 2.16.840.1.666.3
```

When signing the cert with ```openssl ca```, i get this:

```
X509v3 Policy Mappings:
   2.16.840.1.999.1:2.16.840.1.666.1, 
   2.16.840.1.999.2:2.16.840.1.777.1, 
   2.16.840.1.999.3:2.16.840.1.666.3
```

So this (first) occurrence of the issuer:subject policy mapping ending in .2 policy is lost in the final certificate:
```2.16.840.1.999.2:2.16.840.1.666.2```

There is a workaround by manually creating the ASN.1 structure of the policyMappings extension and then adding it to the profile (sample python script attached to do this, i've also done similar with ```openssl asn1parse -genconf```):

```
2.5.29.33 = DER:30503012060760864801876701060760864801851a013012060760864801876702060760864801851a0230120607608648018767020607608648018609013012060760864801876703060760864801851a03
```

Which correctly produces the following:
```
X509v3 Policy Mappings:
2.16.840.1.999.1:2.16.840.1.666.1,
2.16.840.1.999.2:2.16.840.1.666.2,
2.16.840.1.999.2:2.16.840.1.777.1,
2.16.840.1.999.3:2.16.840.1.666.3
```

But this is tedious and having the extension be able to parse multiple occurrences of one issuer policy to multiple subject policies would be very helpful.

Thanks!

[openssl-create-policyMappings-manually.py](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/27406807/openssl-create-policyMappings-manually.py)

Let me quote from openssl-env(7):
```
* OPENSSL_TRACE
...
The value of this environment variable is a comma-separated list of names, with the following available:
* OPENSSL_RUNNING_UNIT_TESTS
...
** TRACE
...
```
The section OPENSSL_RUNNING_UNIT_TESTS is inserted in section OPENSSL_TRACE.
Note TRACE is with correct indentation.


It seems to me that  OPENSSL_RUNNING_UNIT_TESTS section should be after OPENSSL_MODULES in 3.3-HEAD and keep alphabetical order even in 3.6+.

items.title: mispaced OPENSSL_RUNNING_UNIT_TESTS section

items.body:
### Summary

The ML-DSA `message-encoding` parameter accepts integer values other than `0` and `1`, and treats them as enabled.

The documentation describes `message-encoding` as an integer parameter where the default value `1` uses Pure ML-DSA Signature Generation, and setting it to `0` does not encode the message.

In OpenSSL 3.5.6, other integer values such as `-1`, `2`, `3`, `255`, and `256` are also accepted. Signatures produced with those values verify successfully when `message-encoding` is set to `1`, but not when it is set to `0`.

This was reproduced for:

- `ML-DSA-44`
- `ML-DSA-65`
- `ML-DSA-87`

It is not clear from the current documentation whether values other than `0` and `1` are intentionally accepted as enabled.

### Minimal reproducer

A minimal standalone reproducer is attached:

- `openssl_mldsa_message_encoding_domain_repro.cpp`

The reproducer generates an ML-DSA key for each parameter set and tests these `message-encoding` values:

```text
-1, 0, 1, 2, 3, 255, 256
```

For each value `v`, it performs:

1. sign with `message-encoding = v`
2. verify with the same value `v`
3. verify with `message-encoding = 0`
4. verify with `message-encoding = 1`

All other inputs are fixed.

### Observed behavior

Tested with OpenSSL 3.5.6.

```text
openssl_version,OpenSSL 3.5.6 7 Apr 2026
algorithm,value,sign_init_ok,sign_ok,verify_same_rc,verify_as_0_rc,verify_as_1_rc,verify_same_init_ok,sig_len,error_stage,error_text,classification
ML-DSA-44,-1,1,1,1,0,1,1,2420,none,none,out_of_domain_behaves_like_1
pair,ML-DSA-44,message_encoding,-1,out_of_domain_value_accepted_as_1
ML-DSA-44,0,1,1,1,1,0,1,2420,none,none,documented_value
ML-DSA-44,1,1,1,1,0,1,1,2420,none,none,documented_value
ML-DSA-44,2,1,1,1,0,1,1,2420,none,none,out_of_domain_behaves_like_1
pair,ML-DSA-44,message_encoding,2,out_of_domain_value_accepted_as_1
ML-DSA-44,3,1,1,1,0,1,1,2420,none,none,out_of_domain_behaves_like_1
pair,ML-DSA-44,message_encoding,3,out_of_domain_value_accepted_as_1
ML-DSA-44,255,1,1,1,0,1,1,2420,none,none,out_of_domain_behaves_like_1
pair,ML-DSA-44,message_encoding,255,out_of_domain_value_accepted_as_1
ML-DSA-44,256,1,1,1,0,1,1,2420,none,none,out_of_domain_behaves_like_1
pair,ML-DSA-44,message_encoding,256,out_of_domain_value_accepted_as_1
ML-DSA-65,-1,1,1,1,0,1,1,3309,none,none,out_of_domain_behaves_like_1
pair,ML-DSA-65,message_encoding,-1,out_of_domain_value_accepted_as_1
ML-DSA-65,0,1,1,1,1,0,1,3309,none,none,documented_value
ML-DSA-65,1,1,1,1,0,1,1,3309,none,none,documented_value
ML-DSA-65,2,1,1,1,0,1,1,3309,none,none,out_of_domain_behaves_like_1
pair,ML-DSA-65,message_encoding,2,out_of_domain_value_accepted_as_1
ML-DSA-65,3,1,1,1,0,1,1,3309,none,none,out_of_domain_behaves_like_1
pair,ML-DSA-65,message_encoding,3,out_of_domain_value_accepted_as_1
ML-DSA-65,255,1,1,1,0,1,1,3309,none,none,out_of_domain_behaves_like_1
pair,ML-DSA-65,message_encoding,255,out_of_domain_value_accepted_as_1
ML-DSA-65,256,1,1,1,0,1,1,3309,none,none,out_of_domain_behaves_like_1
pair,ML-DSA-65,message_encoding,256,out_of_domain_value_accepted_as_1
ML-DSA-87,-1,1,1,1,0,1,1,4627,none,none,out_of_domain_behaves_like_1
pair,ML-DSA-87,message_encoding,-1,out_of_domain_value_accepted_as_1
ML-DSA-87,0,1,1,1,1,0,1,4627,none,none,documented_value
ML-DSA-87,1,1,1,1,0,1,1,4627,none,none,documented_value
ML-DSA-87,2,1,1,1,0,1,1,4627,none,none,out_of_domain_behaves_like_1
pair,ML-DSA-87,message_encoding,2,out_of_domain_value_accepted_as_1
ML-DSA-87,3,1,1,1,0,1,1,4627,none,none,out_of_domain_behaves_like_1
pair,ML-DSA-87,message_encoding,3,out_of_domain_value_accepted_as_1
ML-DSA-87,255,1,1,1,0,1,1,4627,none,none,out_of_domain_behaves_like_1
pair,ML-DSA-87,message_encoding,255,out_of_domain_value_accepted_as_1
ML-DSA-87,256,1,1,1,0,1,1,4627,none,none,out_of_domain_behaves_like_1
pair,ML-DSA-87,message_encoding,256,out_of_domain_value_accepted_as_1
result,potential_issue_detected
exit_code=1
```

### Result summary

For documented values:

```text
message-encoding = 0:
  sign succeeds
  verify with 0 succeeds
  verify with 1 fails

message-encoding = 1:
  sign succeeds
  verify with 1 succeeds
  verify with 0 fails
```

For out-of-domain values:

```text
message-encoding = -1, 2, 3, 255, 256:
  sign succeeds
  verify with the same value succeeds
  verify with 0 fails
  verify with 1 succeeds
```

This indicates that these non-0/1 values are accepted and behave like `message-encoding = 1`.

### Expected behavior

The behavior for `message-encoding` values other than `0` and `1` should be explicit.

Expected behavior would be one of the following:

1. values other than `0` and `1` are rejected; or
2. the documentation states that `message-encoding` is treated as a boolean-like parameter where any nonzero value enables Pure ML-DSA Signature Generation.

The current behavior accepts non-0/1 values and treats them as enabled, but this behavior is not clear from the documented `0`/`1` cases.

[env_openssl_3_5_6_sanitized.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/27443200/env_openssl_3_5_6_sanitized.txt)
[openssl_evp_mac_hmac_null0_repro.cpp](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/27443201/openssl_evp_mac_hmac_null0_repro.cpp)

labels.description: The issue was opened to report a bug
labels.name: issue: bug report

items.title: ML-DSA message-encoding accepts non-0/1 values as enabled

items.body:
### Environment
- OpenSSL 4.0.0 (Shining Light Productions build for Windows)
- Windows 11 Pro 25H2
- Yubico PIV Tool PKCS#11 module (`libykcs11.dll`)
- Provider PKCS#11 libp11-0.4.18
- YubiKey 5C NFC (PIV)
- Same hardware and PKCS#11 module work correctly with OpenSSL 3.6.2

### Summary
Using the PKCS#11 provider to perform any signing operation (certificate signing, CRL signing, or digest signing) causes OpenSSL 4.0.0 to crash silently on Windows.

No output is produced, no error is displayed, and the output file remains empty (0 bytes).  
Windows Event Viewer reports a crash in `libcrypto-4-x64.dll`.

The same commands work correctly with **OpenSSL 3.6.2**, so this appears to be a **regression**.

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Insert a YubiKey with a PIV private key in slot 82 (id=05).
2. Run one of the following commands:

#### Minimal digest signing test
```
echo "test" | openssl dgst -sha384 -sign "pkcs11:id=%05?pin-source=C:\Users\user\PIN"
```

#### X.509 certificate signing test
```
openssl x509 -req -in leaf.csr -CA intermediate.pem -CAkey "pkcs11:id=%05?pin-source=C:\Users\user\PIN" -out leaf.pem
```

#### CRL generation test
```
openssl ca -config intermediate.cnf -gencrl -out crl.pem
```

### Expected Result
- OpenSSL signs using the PKCS#11 private key, **or**
- OpenSSL returns a clear error message.

### Actual Result
- No output
- No error message
- Output file is created but empty (0 bytes)
- Windows Event Viewer shows:

```
Faulting module: C:\Program Files\OpenSSL-Win64\bin\libcrypto-4-x64.dll
```

### Additional Notes
- The PKCS#11 provider loads correctly.
- The private key is detected.
- PIN authentication succeeds.
- Only the signing operation triggers the crash.
- All commands work correctly with **OpenSSL 3.6.2**, using the same PKCS#11 module and the same hardware.

This suggests a regression in the PKCS#11 signing path in OpenSSL 4.0.0 on Windows

labels.name: issue: bug report

items.title: Crash in libcrypto-4-x64.dll when using PKCS#11 provider for signing (regression vs OpenSSL 3.6.2)

items.body:
OpenSSL HTTP client accepts request components derived from URLs and serializes them into outbound HTTP requests. Those components may contain CR or LF characters. If such characters are present, they can alter the generated HTTP request by injecting additional header lines or splitting the request.

This can occur when an application passes an externally supplied URL to OpenSSL HTTP helper APIs. It can also occur indirectly when an application fetches certificate URLs, such as OCSP responder URLs, AIA locations or CRL distribution points, using OpenSSL provided HTTP functionality.

OpenSSL default certificate verification path doesn't initiate HTTP retrieval of AIA, OCSP or CRL URLs. However, OpenSSL provides helper paths that applications can use for this purpose. In applications that opt into fetching certificate URLs, attacker controlled URL components from certificate metadata can reach OpenSSL HTTP request generation path. The same issue is reachable through direct use of the public HTTP APIs with externally supplied URLs.

Depending on how the OpenSSL HTTP client is used, this may allow HTTP header injection or HTTP request splitting. In proxy configurations, the same primitive may affect the request sent through the proxy.

A proposed fix will be submitted as a pull request. The intended fix is to reject CR and LF characters at the HTTP request serialization boundary, while keeping URL parsing behavior unchanged.
items.title: HTTP client should reject CR/LF in request components
