items.body: The use of the Te1, Te2, and Te3 macros, for example at line 676, potentially invokes ub. For example, consider `Te1[(s1 >> 8) & 0xff]`. For the sake of argument, assume that `(s1 >> 8) & 0xff` resolves to 255. In that case, the macro expands to `(u32)((u64 *)((u8 *)Te + 3))[255]`. The Te array contains 255 u64 values. Thus, this part of the expression `((u64 *)((u8 *)Te + 3))[255]` loads a u64 value from that array, the top 3 bytes of which are from off the end of the array, which is definitely ub. Of course, then it is cast to `u32` and those four bytes are discarded, which is why the ub is in practice harmless. In fact, the compiler probably creates assembly that only loads the lower 4 bytes anyways, which is why this has never caused a crash if, for example, the Te array was at the very end of a page and the next page didn't have read permissions set or was otherwise invalid. Still, it might be worthwhile cleaning it up into a memcpy or at least a 32 bit load, just in case the compiler ever gets sufficiently clever to start assuming that the bytes of s1 are always <= 254.

```
# Ignore strange behaviour setup - It is jut to prepare the next main code block to run

$ openssl req -x509 -sha512 -nodes -days 365 -newkey ed448 \

  -keyout ed448.key -out ed448.crt

# Convert in PKCS#12 format

$ openssl pkcs12 -export -inkey ed448.key -in ed448.crt -out ed448.p12 \
  -macalg SHA512 -iter 100000 \
  -pbmac1_pbkdf2 -pbmac1_pbkdf2_md SHA512 \

  -passout pass:

# Read Client p12 certificate
$ openssl pkcs12 -info -in ed448.p12 \

And this is the *relevant* `-info` output:

MAC: PBMAC1 using PBKDF2, Iteration 100000

PKCS7 Encrypted data: PBES2, PBKDF2, AES-256-CBC, Iteration 100000, PRF hmacWithSHA256

The only `PRF` able to be used is **hmacWithSHA256**.

We found a reproducible out-of-bounds read in EC_GROUP_new_from_params() when OSSL_PKEY_PARAM_EC_GENERATOR is an octet string of length 0.

- buf[0] is read before validating data_size > 0 in crypto/ec/ec_lib.c (around line 1720).
- Reachable paths is direct EC_GROUP_new_from_params() and provider import via EVP_PKEY_fromdata() (ossl_ec_group_fromdata / ec_kmgmt path).
- It can process crash / DoS (ASan reports heap-buffer-overflow, read of size 1).
- Not a side-channel/hardware class; this appears in-scope under OpenSSL threat model.

items.title: OOB read/crash in EC explicit-params import with empty EC_GENERATOR

items.body:
## Summary

I found a runtime issue in the `openssl ocsp` responder HTTP server path.

If a client connects to `openssl ocsp -port ...` and closes the TCP connection before sending any HTTP request bytes, the responder can become permanently stuck on that closed connection. The process remains alive and the listening port remains open, but subsequent clients do not receive responses and time out.

This appears to be caused by `http_server_get_asn1_req()` returning `0` on EOF from the first request-line read without freeing/clearing the accepted client BIO.

## Version

```
$ LD_LIBRARY_PATH=. ./apps/openssl version -a
OpenSSL 4.1.0-dev  (Library: OpenSSL 4.1.0-dev )
built on: Sat May  9 06:07:14 2026 UTC
platform: linux-x86_64
options:  bn(64,64)
compiler: gcc -fPIC -pthread -m64 -Wa,--noexecstack -Wall -O3 -DL_ENDIAN -DOPENSSL_PIC -DNDEBUG
OPENSSLDIR: "/usr/local/ssl"
MODULESDIR: "/usr/local/lib64/ossl-modules"
Seeding source: os-specific
CPUINFO: OPENSSL_ia32cap=0xfffa32235f8bffff:0x184007a4219c27ab:0x00000810bc104410:0x0000000000000000:0x0000000000000000
```

This was tested with my own build from the OpenSSL source tree, using the existing `apps/openssl`, `libssl.so`, and `libcrypto.so` build artifacts.

## Relevant code

In `apps/lib/http_server.c`, after accepting a connection:

```c
if (BIO_do_accept(acbio) <= 0)
    return ret;

*pcbio = cbio = BIO_pop(acbio);
...
len = BIO_gets(cbio, reqbuf, sizeof(reqbuf));
if (len == 0)
    return ret;
```

At this point `ret` is still `0`. Returning `0` makes callers treat the condition as "no request yet", but `*pcbio` still points to the accepted connection BIO which has already reached EOF.

In `apps/ocsp.c`, the responder loop handles this as retryable:

```c
res = do_responder(&req, &cbio, acbio, req_timeout);
if (res == 0)
    goto redo_accept;
```

Since `cbio` was not cleared, the next iteration does not accept a new connection. It retries the existing closed connection instead.

## Reproduction

The following reproducer starts a minimal OCSP responder, verifies a normal request works, then triggers the issue by opening and closing one idle connection before sending any bytes.

```
$ mkdir -p /tmp/openssl-ocsp-eof
$ : > /tmp/openssl-ocsp-eof/index.txt
$ LD_LIBRARY_PATH=. OPENSSL_CONF=./apps/openssl.cnf ./apps/openssl req -x509 -newkey rsa:2048 -nodes \
    -keyout /tmp/openssl-ocsp-eof/key.pem \
    -out /tmp/openssl-ocsp-eof/cert.pem \
    -subj /CN=ocsp-poc \
    -days 1
```

Start the responder:

```
$ LD_LIBRARY_PATH=. OPENSSL_CONF=./apps/openssl.cnf ./apps/openssl ocsp \
    -port 18081 \
    -index /tmp/openssl-ocsp-eof/index.txt \
    -rsigner /tmp/openssl-ocsp-eof/cert.pem \
    -rkey /tmp/openssl-ocsp-eof/key.pem \
    -CA /tmp/openssl-ocsp-eof/cert.pem \
    -ignore_err
ACCEPT 0.0.0.0:18081 PID=...
ocsp: waiting for OCSP client connections...
```

Baseline request succeeds:

```
$ python3 - <<'PY'
import socket
s = socket.create_connection(("127.0.0.1", 18081), timeout=2)
s.sendall(b"GET / HTTP/1.0\r\n\r\n")
s.settimeout(2)
buf = b""
while True:
    data = s.recv(4096)
    if not data:
        break
    buf += data
print(buf.decode("latin1", "replace"))
s.close()
PY
HTTP/1.0 200 OK
```

Trigger the issue:

```
$ python3 - <<'PY'
import socket
s = socket.create_connection(("127.0.0.1", 18081), timeout=2)
s.close()
print("idle connection closed")
PY
idle connection closed
```

After that, normal requests time out:

```
$ python3 - <<'PY'
import socket
s = socket.create_connection(("127.0.0.1", 18081), timeout=2)
s.sendall(b"GET / HTTP/1.0\r\n\r\n")
s.settimeout(2)
buf = b""
try:
    while True:
        data = s.recv(4096)
        if not data:
            break
        buf += data
except TimeoutError:
    print("timed out after receiving", len(buf), "bytes")
s.close()
PY
timed out after receiving 0 bytes
```

Repeating the final request continues to time out. The responder process remains alive, but it no longer processes new application-layer requests.

## Expected behavior

An accepted client connection that closes before sending a request line should be closed and discarded. The responder should then continue accepting and processing new client connections.

## Actual behavior

The EOF condition is returned as `0` without clearing the client BIO. The OCSP responder loop retries the stale closed BIO and does not return to the accept path, causing subsequent clients to hang.

## Suggested fix direction

When `BIO_gets(cbio, ...)` returns `0` immediately after accepting a connection, `http_server_get_asn1_req()` should clean up the client BIO and clear `*pcbio`, or otherwise return a status that lets callers distinguish "connection closed" from "no request yet".

For example, the `len == 0` path should not directly `return ret` while leaving `*pcbio` set.

labels.description: The issue was opened to report a bug
labels.name: issue: bug report

items.title: `openssl ocsp` responder stops accepting new requests after idle client closes before sending request line

items.body:
**OpenSSL version:** master (4.1.0-dev), commit `5199c5b98a`. Same code pattern present in openssl-3.2.0 through openssl-3.6.1 (introduced in commit `6399d7856c`). Absent in 3.1.x.

**OS/Hardware:** Ubuntu 22.04, ARM64 (Neoverse-N1, Azure Standard_D4ps_v5)

**Summary:**

The two scalar multiplication functions in `crypto/ec/ecp_sm2p256.c` have data-dependent branches on the scalar value, unlike the equivalent P-256 code in `ecp_nistz256.c`.

`ecp_sm2p256_point_P_mul_by_scalar()` (lines 370–414) processes the scalar in 4-bit nibble windows with an `if (index)` branch that skips `point_add` when a nibble is zero:

```c
for (i = 64 - 1; i >= 0; --i) {
    index = (k[i / 16] >> (4 * (i % 16))) & mask;
    // ...
    if (index)
        ecp_sm2p256_point_add(R, R, &precomputed[index]);
}
```

The `init` flag logic at lines 400–404 also branches on the scalar.

`ecp_sm2p256_point_G_mul_by_scalar()` (lines 335–364) has the same pattern with 8-bit byte windows.

**P-256 comparison:** `ecp_nistz256.c` handles this with `ecp_nistz256_gather_w5`/`w7` (constant-time table lookup) and unconditional point operations in the loop body.

**Context:** CVE-2025-9231 (commit dff94dba75) fixed timing in the modular inversion path (`get_affine`/`field_inv`/`inv_mod_ord`) in the same file. The scalar multiplication loop was not part of that fix.

**Note on complete fix:** `ecp_sm2p256_point_add()` (early returns at lines 286, 294, 302), `ecp_sm2p256_point_double()` (line 186), and `ecp_sm2p256_point_add_affine()` (early returns at lines 224, 243) all have identity-dependent branches that would need CT variants for a complete fix.

**Affected call sites:**
- `sm2_crypt.c:360` — `EC_POINT_mul(group, C1, NULL, C1, EC_KEY_get0_private_key(key), ctx)` — SM2 decryption (static private key)
- `sm2_sign.c:295` — `EC_POINT_mul(group, kG, k, NULL, NULL, ctx)` — SM2 signing (ephemeral nonce)

**Platforms:** The optimized SM2 path compiles on ARM64 and RISC-V. x86_64 does not compile this optimized `ecp_sm2p256` path.

**Observed behavior (ARM64 Neoverse):**
- Direct `EC_POINT_mul`: r = -0.9828, slope = -389 ns/zero nibble (100 keys, Fisher-Yates shuffled)
- Full SM2 decrypt path (`EVP_PKEY_decrypt`): r = -0.9752 (shuffled crafted keys), r = -0.9829 (random keygen keys)
- x86_64 control: optimized symbol absent, no comparable signal (r = 0.152)

[Measurement data and detailed write-up](https://abhinavagarwal07.github.io/posts/openssl-sm2-timing/?utm_campaign=openssl&src=gh)

labels.description: The issue was opened to report a bug
labels.name: issue: bug report

items.title: OpenSSL 4.1.0-dev build failed on TCC compiler

items.body:
### Problem

While implementing a custom async BIO with Linux kTLS support, I ran into a TLS 1.3 KeyUpdate + kTLS RX problem.

As far as I understand the Linux kTLS RX model, the kernel keeps the currently installed RX crypto context. It does not keep a set of old/new RX keys and does not try multiple keys for incoming records.

When the currently installed RX key is no longer valid for the next TLS 1.3 record epoch, recvmsg() may return:

    -EKEYEXPIRED

At that point userspace is expected to install the next RX key with:

    setsockopt(fd, SOL_TLS, TLS_RX, new_crypto_info, ...)

and then retry receiving.

### Expected flow

For kTLS RX, the expected flow seems to be:

    recvmsg() -> -EKEYEXPIRED
    OpenSSL record layer handles TLS 1.3 KeyUpdate state
    OpenSSL derives the next RX traffic secret
    OpenSSL calls BIO_CTRL_SET_KTLS for the RX direction again
    BIO installs the new TLS_RX key with setsockopt()
    read is retried

### Current concern

I do not see where this flow is implemented in OpenSSL’s kTLS record layer.

A custom BIO can propagate EKEYEXPIRED from recvmsg(), but the actual fix has to be in the SSL/record layer: BIO cannot derive TLS 1.3 traffic secrets or drive KeyUpdate by itself.

For TX, an async BIO can preserve ordering by placing TLS_TX setsockopt into the same outbound queue between old-key writes and new-key writes.

For RX, however, TLS_RX update has to be a synchronous/control barrier before the next recvmsg submit/retry.

### Related issue

Related: #16307 discussed TLS 1.3 kTLS traffic secret updates.

Related: #18276 appears to work around kTLS instability by forcing a fallback to the userspace record path. That may avoid the immediate failure, but it does not appear to implement the actual Linux kTLS RX KeyUpdate flow described above.

### Question

Is TLS 1.3 kTLS RX KeyUpdate intended to be supported in OpenSSL?

If yes, what is the expected mechanism for a custom BIO to trigger the RX key update after recvmsg() returns EKEYEXPIRED?

Specifically, where should the following flow happen?

    EKEYEXPIRED from BIO/recvmsg
    -> derive/update RX traffic secret
    -> re-install TLS_RX
    -> retry read

labels.description: The issue is not considered a bug
labels.name: resolved: not a bug

items.title: TLS 1.3 kTLS RX KeyUpdate handling for custom BIOs / EKEYEXPIRED path

For other issues:

items.body:
I receive the following warning out of obj_dat.c when compiling with `CHARSET_EBCDIC` defined:

```
In function 'OBJ_bsearch_ex_':
712:16: warning: assignment discards 'const' qualifier from pointer target type [-Wdiscarded-qualifiers] p1 = &(base_[i * size]);
```

I believe `p1` should be defined as a `const char *` instead of a `char *` which should resolve the compiler warning issue.

Also variables `l` and `h` in the same code block appear to be unused I think they can be removed.

items.body:
Some logic was recently put into crypto/initthread.c in the 3.5 and 3.6 branches (though not master or 4.0) to fix https://github.com/openssl/openssl/issues/29077 in commit https://github.com/openssl/openssl/commit/66f92cfb925e5c4c830ab65cf738ab49c5e553a4 of pull request https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/29182 by ensuring that the OpenSSL thread-local variable destructor_key is initialized before any other OpenSSL thread-local variables.

The logic works like so:
1. All thread-local variable creation in CRYPTO_THREAD_init_local goes through ossl_init_thread first to ensure destructor_key is initialized: https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/286ddeaac037533bbdce65b3c689e3f7ffebf0f6/crypto/threads_pthread.c#L735-L736 (likewise for threads_win.c and threads_none.c)
2. ossl_init_thread uses RUN_ONCE to do CRYPTO_THREAD_init_local to initialize destructor_key once: https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/286ddeaac037533bbdce65b3c689e3f7ffebf0f6/crypto/initthread.c#L216-L218
3. ossl_init_thread also sets a global variable recursion_guard to the current thread while it works so that the initial CRYPTO_THREAD_init_local to initialize destructor_key doesn't lead to infinite regress: https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/286ddeaac037533bbdce65b3c689e3f7ffebf0f6/crypto/initthread.c#L230-L235

This approach has led to several problems:
- On pthread-based platforms, the initialization of recursion_guard to anything other than (a) a valid thread ID (which can only be obtained from pthread_create or pthread_self, as far as I'm aware) or (b) PTHREAD_NULL is undefined: https://pubs.opengroup.org/onlinepubs/9799919799.2024edition/functions/pthread_equal.html
  - On NetBSD, this led to actual crashes: https://github.com/openssl/openssl/issues/29828
  - Once patched to use (pthread_t)0 instead of (pthread_t)-1 as in https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/29952, although it doesn't crash with SIGSEGV, it still produces diagnostics of the UB in pthread_equal: https://gnats.NetBSD.org/60244
    > After the openssl 3.5.6 pullup, programs that invoke openssl (in various ways)
    > cause pthread warnings to be emitted when PTHREAD_DIAGASSERT=Ae is set:
    > 
    > ```
    > Error detected by libpthread: Invalid thread.
    > Detected by file "/src/hg/11src/lib/libpthread/pthread.c", line 772, function "pthread_equal".
    > ```
    > 
    > normally, this is just screen chatter, but will dump core with
    > PTHREAD_DIAGASSERT=a set.
  - (NetBSD also doesn't have PTHREAD_NULL yet, which is new in POSIX.1-2024: https://gnats.NetBSD.org/60254)
- On Tandem Nonstop, the initialization of recursion_guard made no sense and failed to build (https://github.com/openssl/openssl/issues/29854).  On MSVC, using (CRYPTO_THREAD_ID)0 caused some performance regression (https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/30296).  So now there is an inline initializer with #ifdefs around it to hard-code various platform-dependent implementation details of the thread ID type: https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/286ddeaac037533bbdce65b3c689e3f7ffebf0f6/crypto/initthread.c#L203-L210
- Access to the recursion_guard variable is inherently racy, as [observed by tsan while drafting the original change](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/issues/29077#issuecomment-3559076280).  While the current approach may be empirically _mostly harmless_ on several platforms tested (though warning fatigue itself is not harmless), this UB is a rake waiting for someone to step on later -- not to mention a certain [incident in OpenSSL history with pacifying diagnostics](https://research.swtch.com/openssl).

---

I suggest that the original problem -- that destructor_key must be initialized first, before any other CRYPTO_THREAD_init_local variables -- can be solved in a much simpler way without any recursion guard:

1. Define a library-internal ossl_thread_init_local (or something, name is not especially important since it's library-internal) in threads_*.c that does the per-platform thing (pthread_key_create on pthread platforms, TlsAlloc on Windows, and so on) _but not_ ossl_init_thread.
2. Use ossl_thread_init_local inside ossl_init_thread to initialize destructor_key.
3. In initthread.c, define a wrapper CRYPTO_THREAD_init_local that just does:
   - ossl_init_thread
   - ossl_thread_init_local

This will guarantee the necessary ordering (initialize destructor_key before any CRYPTO_THREAD_init_local can create other keys), will avoid the UB and nest of platform #ifdefs that has plagued the current approach of recursion_guard, and will make the static call graph simpler to understand.
items.title: crypto/initthread.c recursion_guard UB

items.title: Failure Detect: 22H2: libcrypto-1_1-x64.dll Crash  (access_violation_c0000005_libcrypto-1_1-x64.dll!unknown)

items.body:
## Description

After upgrading from OpenSSL 3.6.1 to 3.6.2 via Homebrew on macOS (arm64), both the `openssl` CLI binary and Ruby's `require 'openssl'` hang indefinitely with no output or error. Rolling back to 3.6.1 resolves the issue immediately.

## Environment

- **OS**: macOS 26.3.1 (Darwin 25.3.0, arm64 / Apple Silicon)
- **OpenSSL**: 3.6.2 (Homebrew) — `libssl.3.dylib` updated 2026-05-14
- **Ruby**: 4.0.1 (arm64-darwin24), openssl extension linked against `/opt/homebrew/opt/openssl@3/lib/libssl.3.dylib`
- **Previous working version**: OpenSSL 3.6.1 (27 Jan 2026)

## Steps to Reproduce

```bash
brew upgrade openssl@3   # upgrades to 3.6.2
openssl version          # hangs — no output, no error
ruby -ropenssl -e "puts OpenSSL::VERSION"   # hangs indefinitely
```

Workaround:
```bash
# Rollback to 3.6.1 (still in Homebrew Cellar)
rm /opt/homebrew/opt/openssl@3
ln -s /opt/homebrew/Cellar/openssl@3/3.6.1 /opt/homebrew/opt/openssl@3
# Now both commands work again
```

## Expected Behavior

`openssl version` should print version string; `require 'openssl'` should load without hanging.

## Notes

- `OPENSSL_CONF=/dev/null` does not fix the hang (ruling out config file issue)
- `OBJC_DISABLE_INITIALIZE_FORK_SAFETY=YES` does not fix it either
- The hang happens before any network activity (during `dlopen` / library initialization)
- `otool -L` shows the extension links against `/opt/homebrew/opt/openssl@3/lib/libssl.3.dylib` and `libcrypto.3.dylib`
items.title: OpenSSL 3.6.2: ruby require 'openssl' and openssl CLI hang indefinitely on macOS arm64 (regression from 3.6.1)

items.body:
<!--
Thank you for your bug report. If this is your first one,
please take the time to read the following lines before posting it.

NOTE:

    If you're asking about how to use OpenSSL, this isn't the right
    forum.  Please see our User Support resources:
    https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/master/SUPPORT.md

Please remember to tell us in what OpenSSL version you found the issue.

For build issues:

    If this is a build issue, please include the configuration output
    as well as a log of all errors.  Don't forget to include the exact
    commands you typed.

    With OpenSSL before 1.1.1, the configuration output comes from the
    configuration command.  With OpenSSL 1.1.1 and on, it's the output
    of `perl configdata.pm --dump`

For other issues:

    If it isn't a build issue, example code or commands to reproduce
    the issue is highly appreciated.
    Also, please remember to tell us if you worked with your own
    OpenSSL build or if it is system provided.

Please remember to put ``` lines before and after any commands plus
output and code, like this:

    ```
    $ echo output output output
    output output output
    ```

    ```
    #include <stdio.h>

    int main() {
        int foo = 1;
        printf("%d\n", foo);
    }
    ```
-->
In the file include/openssl/obj_mac.h: the OID for 1.3.6.1.5.5.7.48.1.8 is assinged to OBJ_id_pkix_OCSP_extendedStatus.

The correct OID for OBJ_id_pkix_OCSP_extendedStatus is 1.3.6.1.5.5.7.48.1.9. And 1.3.6.1.5.5.7.48.1.8 is for id-pkix-ocsp-pref-sig-algs.

Below the definition from RFC 6960:
```
-- Object Identifiers

id-kp-OCSPSigning            OBJECT IDENTIFIER ::= { id-kp 9 }
id-pkix-ocsp                 OBJECT IDENTIFIER ::= id-ad-ocsp
id-pkix-ocsp-basic           OBJECT IDENTIFIER ::= { id-pkix-ocsp 1 }
id-pkix-ocsp-nonce           OBJECT IDENTIFIER ::= { id-pkix-ocsp 2 }
id-pkix-ocsp-crl             OBJECT IDENTIFIER ::= { id-pkix-ocsp 3 }
id-pkix-ocsp-response        OBJECT IDENTIFIER ::= { id-pkix-ocsp 4 }
id-pkix-ocsp-nocheck         OBJECT IDENTIFIER ::= { id-pkix-ocsp 5 }
id-pkix-ocsp-archive-cutoff  OBJECT IDENTIFIER ::= { id-pkix-ocsp 6 }
id-pkix-ocsp-service-locator OBJECT IDENTIFIER ::= { id-pkix-ocsp 7 }
id-pkix-ocsp-pref-sig-algs   OBJECT IDENTIFIER ::= { id-pkix-ocsp 8 }
id-pkix-ocsp-extended-revoke OBJECT IDENTIFIER ::= { id-pkix-ocsp 9 }
```

labels.name: issue: bug report

items.title: Incorrect OID mapping for OBJ_id_pkix_OCSP_extendedStatus, OBJ_id_pkix_OCSP_valid, and OBJ_id_pkix_OCSP_path

items.body:
When compiling OpenSSL (master branch, Strawberry Perl 5.42.2.1, nasm 3.01) on Windows, the following error is reported:
```
'-x' is not recognized as an internal or external command,
operable program or batch file.
```

To reproduce, open a "x64 Native Tools Command Prompt for VS" (VS 2026)
```
set path=%ProgramFiles%\nasm\;%path%
nasm --version
perl Configure VC-WIN64A
nmake /nologo -f makefile build_libs > build.release.log
```

When using the `no-asm` configuration option, the issue cannot be reproduced.
```
perl Configure VC-WIN64A no-asm
``` 

The cause can be found in the asm generating perl scripts. E.g. crypto\aes\asm\aesni-sha1-x86_64.pl
```
$avx=1 if (`$ENV{CC} -Wa,-v -c -o /dev/null -x assembler /dev/null 2>&1`
		=~ /GNU assembler version ([2-9]\.[0-9]+)/ &&
	   $1>=2.19);
```
The CC environment variable is not defined in the Visual Studio Command Prompt.

These checks for AVX in every single asm generator are broken and should probably be replaced with a better mechanism.

labels.description: The issue was opened to report a bug
labels.name: issue: bug report

items.title: openssl latest master got compiler warnings and breakup

items.body:
### Summary

`EVP_chacha20_poly1305` accepts AAD after plaintext has already been processed, and the resulting authentication tag depends on the EVP update order.

The reproducer compares a canonical AEAD update order:

```text
AAD1 -> AAD2 -> PT1 -> PT2
```

with a late/interleaved AAD order:

```text
AAD1 -> PT1 -> AAD2 -> PT2
```

For `ChaCha20-Poly1305`, the late/interleaved order is accepted. It produces the same ciphertext as the canonical order, but a different tag. The tag is also different from the no-AAD tag. Verification succeeds only when decryption repeats the same late/interleaved update order, and fails when decryption uses the canonical AAD-before-plaintext order.

For comparison:

- `AES-128-GCM` rejects the same late AAD update.
- `AES-128-OCB` accepts the same late AAD update, but produces the same tag as the canonical order.

This report is about the accepted late-AAD update order and the resulting order-dependent authentication tag. It is not claiming that an invalid tag is accepted.

### Minimal reproducer

A minimal standalone reproducer is attached:

- `openssl_chacha20_poly1305_late_aad_order_repro.cpp`

The reproducer tests three AEAD ciphers:

- `AES-128-GCM`
- `ChaCha20-Poly1305`
- `AES-128-OCB`

For each cipher, it compares:

```text
canonical order:
  AAD1 -> AAD2 -> PT1 -> PT2

late/interleaved order:
  AAD1 -> PT1 -> AAD2 -> PT2
```

It then checks whether the late/interleaved output:

- is accepted during encryption;
- has the same ciphertext as the canonical output;
- has the same tag as the canonical output;
- has the same tag as a no-AAD output;
- verifies under canonical AAD order;
- verifies under the same late/interleaved AAD order.

### Observed behavior

Tested with OpenSSL 3.5.6.

```text
openssl_version,OpenSSL 3.5.6 7 Apr 2026
cipher,late_sequence,canonical_enc_ok,late_enc_ok,late_fail_index,same_ct_as_canonical,same_tag_as_canonical,same_tag_as_noaad,canonical_verify_late_ok,same_order_verify_late_ok,suspicious,ct_len,tag_len,canonical_tag_prefix8,late_tag_prefix8,noaad_tag_prefix8,late_fail_stage,late_error_text,canonical_verify_stage,same_order_verify_stage
AES-128-GCM,AAD1_PT1_AAD2_PT2,1,0,2,0,0,0,0,0,0,11,16,6bc0194ffccffc8f,0000000000000000,01e63c5370ace9ba,EncryptUpdate_AAD,error:1C800066:Provider routines::cipher operation failed,input_not_ok,input_not_ok
ChaCha20-Poly1305,AAD1_PT1_AAD2_PT2,1,1,-1,1,0,0,0,1,1,24,16,24d1bb1401466d03,e6293342e7b51be6,1ca9755ecc5a1c4b,none,none,DecryptFinal,none
AES-128-OCB,AAD1_PT1_AAD2_PT2,1,1,-1,1,1,0,1,1,0,24,16,daa2d63aed63f418,daa2d63aed63f418,3f6518d8b99200a7,none,none,none,none
exit_code=1
```

### Result summary

For `AES-128-GCM`, the late AAD update is rejected:

```text
AES-128-GCM:
  canonical_enc_ok = 1
  late_enc_ok = 0
  late_fail_stage = EncryptUpdate_AAD
  late_error_text = error:1C800066:Provider routines::cipher operation failed
```

For `ChaCha20-Poly1305`, the late AAD update is accepted and produces an order-dependent tag:

```text
ChaCha20-Poly1305:
  canonical_enc_ok = 1
  late_enc_ok = 1
  same_ct_as_canonical = 1
  same_tag_as_canonical = 0
  same_tag_as_noaad = 0
  canonical_verify_late_ok = 0
  same_order_verify_late_ok = 1
  suspicious = 1
```

This means the sequence:

```text
AAD1 -> PT1 -> AAD2 -> PT2
```

is accepted by `ChaCha20-Poly1305`. The ciphertext is the same as in the canonical order, but the tag is different. The late-order tag is not accepted when verification uses the canonical AAD-before-plaintext order, but it is accepted when verification repeats the same late/interleaved order.

For `AES-128-OCB`, the late AAD update is accepted, but the result is canonical-equivalent:

```text
AES-128-OCB:
  canonical_enc_ok = 1
  late_enc_ok = 1
  same_ct_as_canonical = 1
  same_tag_as_canonical = 1
  canonical_verify_late_ok = 1
  same_order_verify_late_ok = 1
  suspicious = 0
```

### Expected behavior

It would be helpful for this behavior to be rejected or clearly documented.

Expected behavior would be one of the following:

1. reject AAD updates after plaintext/ciphertext processing has started, as `AES-128-GCM` does; or
2. document that `ChaCha20-Poly1305` supports this EVP update order and treats the update order as part of the authenticated transcript.

The current behavior accepts late/interleaved AAD for `ChaCha20-Poly1305` and produces a tag that verifies only when decryption repeats the same late/interleaved update order.

[env_openssl_3_5_6_sanitized.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/27792775/env_openssl_3_5_6_sanitized.txt)
[openssl_chacha20_poly1305_late_aad_order_repro.cpp](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/27792776/openssl_chacha20_poly1305_late_aad_order_repro.cpp)

items.title: ChaCha20-Poly1305 accepts AAD after plaintext update with order-dependent tags

items.body:
Hello,

Unfortunately, commit 7836b7d from PR #31159 appears to break QUIC connections with SNI for non-default virtual servers in nginx.

The reason is that QUIC transport parameters are not provided in the handshake after switching SSL_CTX on SSL connections. In particular, the OSSL_FUNC_SSL_QUIC_TLS_GOT_TRANSPORT_PARAMS callback isn't called, which appears to be the same issue that led to #27706.

As a repro note, this can be seen in our CI/CD:
https://github.com/nginx/nginx/actions/runs/25814985211/job/75841007764
The issue is visible on the "ossl35", "ossl36", and "oss40" builders, which build nginx with the latest OpenSSL revision on the corresponding branch. It is caught by the h3_ssl_sni.t and h3_ssl_reject_handshake.t tests for QUIC with SNI from nginx-tests:
https://github.com/nginx/nginx-tests/

As an aside, I may be missing something, but it is not clear to me how this correlates with openssl/openssl@dc84829, which was mentioned in the PR, or what exactly was meant by "the nginx bug was already fixed".

If the reversal is still considered correct, any hints on how SNI should be properly handled in this case would be very welcome.

labels.description: The issue/pr is a regression from previous released version
labels.name: severity: regression

items.title: Comments on PR #31159

items.body:
OpenSSL, when used with web servers such as NGINX, in its default configuration, will reject a configured server leaf certificate when the certificate's `signatureAlgorithm` is unknown to OpenSSL, even though a TLS server does not need to verify or otherwise process the signature on its own leaf certificate in order to present it to clients.

This prevents server deployments from experimenting with or deploying new X.509 certificate signature algorithm identifiers before OpenSSL has native recognition for those identifiers. It also requires users to update their server OpenSSL's to use new certificate signature algorithms, even though only the client should need to be updated.

For example, right now with OpenSSL 3.5 + NGINX stable, you cannot configure it to serve an ML-DSA-44 key with a certificate signed by an ML-DSA-44 MTC cosigner, but you can configure it to serve an ML-DSA-44 key with a certificate signed by an ML-DSA-44 "chonky" X.509 CA.

In an ideal scenario, clients could migrate their server configurations transparently to an MTC CA configuration without OpenSSL on the server needing to know anything about the MTC CA itself.

## Expected behavior

OpenSSL operating a server, by default, should accept any leaf certificate so long as:
- the certificate is syntactically valid DER/PEM
- the configured private key corresponds to the certificate's subject public key
- the key type is usable for TLS server authentication

It should not need to understand the certificate _signature_ algorithm. It only needs to be able to sign with the _key algorithm_ in the certificate.

## Actual behavior

When loading a server leaf certificate whose `signatureAlgorithm` uses an unrecognized OID, OpenSSL rejects the certificate during server configuration loading with an error such as:

```text
SSL_CTX_use_certificate(...) failed (...:SSL routines::ca md too weak)
```

This is incorrect. The server should not have a perspective on the strength of the signature _from the CA_, only the signature _generated by the server during the handshake_ (i.e. during Server Key Exchange), which is solely dependent on the _key_ in the certificate, not the signature on the certificate.

## Workaround

You can use an `openssl.cnf` that looks something like this and ensuring `$OPENSSL_CONF` is set in the environment running NGINX.

```
openssl_conf = openssl_init

[openssl_init]
ssl_conf = ssl_sect

[ssl_sect]
system_default = system_default_sect

[system_default_sect]
CipherString = DEFAULT:@SECLEVEL=0
```

In NGINX, `ssl_conf_command CipherString DEFAULT:@SECLEVEL=0;` doesn't work because it's called too late in initialization. 

This is presumably well-beyond the complexity of an administrator who is not a PKI expert, and is simply adding / switch ACME profiles to one that gets a standalone MTC.
items.title: Server-side OpenSSL rejects leaf certificates with unknown signature algorithms

items.body: Should the `Configure` script not support 4.0 / 4.0.0 in the `--api` option, like 3.0 and previous versions are?

items.title: Configure --api 4.0 missing

items.body:
Documentation states:

> OCSP_basic_verify() returns 1 on success, 0 on verification not successful, or -1 on a fatal error such as malloc failure.

Looking at the implementation, this code path will result in returning 2 if `OCSP_NOSIGS` is not set:

https://github.com/openbsd/src/blob/d74df432a3524e8c2670a511e2a3ff67e3b7e706/lib/libcrypto/ocsp/ocsp_vfy.c#L95-L96

because `OCSP_NOVERIFY` is set by that code path and the return value will simply be returned.

labels.description: The issue reports errors in (or missing) documentation
labels.name: issue: documentation

items.body:
When building OpenSSL 4.0.0 for 32bit x86 ABI by using the `-m32` flag with GCC v15.2.1 on a CPU that does support AVX2, the build fails with and without patch da2fa6d applied:

```
x86_64-pc-linux-gnu-gcc -m32 -mfpmath=sse  -I. -Iinclude -Iproviders/common/include -Iproviders/implementations/include -I../openssl-4.0.0 -I../openssl-4.0.0/include -I../openssl-4.0.0/providers/common/include -I../openssl-4.0.0/providers/implementations/include  -DAES_ASM -DCMLL_ASM -DDES_ASM -DECP_NISTZ256_ASM -DGHASH_ASM -DMD5_ASM -DOPENSSL_BN_ASM_GF2m -DOPENSSL_BN_ASM_MONT -DOPENSSL_BN_ASM_PART_WORDS -DOPENSSL_CPUID_OBJ -DOPENSSL_IA32_SSE2 -DPOLY1305_ASM -DRC4_ASM -DRMD160_ASM -DSHA1_ASM -DSHA256_ASM -DSHA512_ASM -DVPAES_ASM -DWHIRLPOOL_ASM -fPIC -pthread -m32 -Wa,--noexecstack -march=native -O2 -pipe -fno-strict-aliasing -Wa,--noexecstack -DL_ENDIAN -DOPENSSL_PIC -DOPENSSLDIR="\"/etc/ssl\"" -DMODULESDIR="\"/usr/lib/ossl-modules\"" -DNDEBUG  -MMD -MF crypto/evp/libcrypto-lib-encode.d.tmp -c -o crypto/evp/libcrypto-lib-encode.o ../openssl-4.0.0/crypto/evp/encode.c
../openssl-4.0.0/crypto/evp/encode.c: In function 'EVP_EncodeUpdate':
../openssl-4.0.0/crypto/evp/encode.c:411:13: error: implicit declaration of function 'encode_base64_avx2' [-Wimplicit-function-declaration]
  411 |         j = encode_base64_avx2(ctx,
      |             ^~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
make[1]: *** [Makefile:12119: crypto/evp/libcrypto-lib-encode.o] Error 1
```

For further details refer [Gentoo bug 972756](https://bugs.gentoo.org/972756)

items.body:
While ASN1_STRING has been documented for some time warning users that
ASN1_STRING data values should not be counted upon to be C strings, the setting functions
still append a NUL byte 'even though it is not strictly necessary' 

Moving to using ASN1_STRING values directly from parsed TBS certificate memory will
absolutely not guarantee that the values are NUL byte terminated. 

We need to rip off this particular band-aid, and ensure our own house is in order
while transitioning others to ensuring theirs is.

labels.description: The issue was opened to request a feature
labels.name: issue: feature request

milestone.description: Items tentatively aimed for the next release.

items.title: Ensure ASN1_STRING does not require an added NUL byte.

items.body:
## Problem
OpenSSL currently checks for session-ID collisions against the internal session cache during server-side session-ID generation. In deployments that use an external session cache, this leaves a gap: collisions against resumable external entries are not visible to OpenSSL at generation time.

This limitation was previously noted in `ssl_sess.c` via a historical `XXX`
comment regarding external cache collision handling.

## Current limitation
- `SSL_has_matching_session_id()` only checks internal cache entries.
- External-cache collisions are not consulted during session-ID generation.
- Reusing `get_session_cb()` is not appropriate:
  - It is part of session lookup/resumption semantics.
  - It is not an existence-check-only API.
  - Using it during generation can alter API meaning and behavior.

## Proposed approach
Add an optional, generation-specific external collision callback:

- `GEN_SESSION_COLLISION_CB`
- `SSL_CTX_set_session_id_collision_cb()`
- `SSL_set_session_id_collision_cb()`

The callback is intended only for lightweight existence checks during
session-ID generation and is not part of resumption lookup semantics.

Return semantics:
- `> 0`: collision exists
- `0`: no collision
- `< 0`: indeterminate; fallback to internal-cache-only behavior

If no callback is set, behavior remains unchanged.

## Compatibility considerations
- Backward compatible by default.
- No change when callback is unset.
- No change to handshake/session negotiation flow.
- No change to `get_session_cb()` contract or call path.

## Concurrency considerations
This does not attempt to provide global atomic reservation semantics across threads/processes.  
Goal is optional external-cache awareness and practical collision-risk reduction.

Implementation refinement included:
- Callback setter APIs write under locks.
- Callback pointers are snapshotted under SSL/CTX read locks before invocation.
- Avoids unlocked reads of mutable callback pointers.

## Non-goals
- No distributed/global atomic session-ID reservation.
- No redesign of session resumption semantics.
- No mandatory external cache integration.

## Testing / validation
- OpenSSL builds successfully with the change.
- Relevant TLS tests pass.
- Existing behavior remains unchanged when callback is unused.
- Documentation added/updated, including new manpage and `CHANGES.md` entry.

## Related PR
- https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/30782

labels.description: The issue was opened to request a feature
labels.name: issue: feature request

items.title: Add optional external session-ID collision callbacks for external cache checks

items.body:
I've recently started using HPKE interfaces, and notices some friction point. To resolve that I would suggest the following additions:
 
### Can't get the suite from the context

`OSSL_HPKE_encap()` and `OSSL_HPKE_seal()` require one to use `OSSL_HPKE_get_public_encap_size()` and `OSSL_HPKE_get_ciphertext_size()` respectively. Both of these take a `OSSL_HPKE_SUITE` argument however, so effectively you can't use a sender context without also carrying around the matching suite. That strongly suggests that the context should be able to give you that information itself. Hence I propose to add the following function for that:

~~~c
OSSL_HPKE_SUITE OSSL_HPKE_get_suite(const OSSL_HPKE_CTX *ctx);
~~~

### Can't get public key size from the suite

When calling `OSSL_HPKE_keygen`, one is supposed to know how big the public key will be. For DHKEM, `OSSL_HPKE_get_public_encap_size()` will return the right value, but that will not be the case for ML-KEM (because `Nenc < Npk`) and hybrid KEMs.

So I propose adding a function that returns the `Npk` of a suite's KEM:
~~~c
size_t OSSL_HPKE_get_public_key_size(OSSL_HPKE_SUITE suite);
~~~

### Can't check if suite supports authenticated modes

Per RFC 9180:
~~~
Note that some KEMs may not support AuthEncap() or AuthDecap().  For
such KEMs, only mode_base or mode_psk are supported.  Future
specifications which define new KEMs MUST indicate whether these
modes are supported.
~~~

And in fact it seems like the upcoming PQC KEM types will not support that (per [draft-ietf-hpke-pq-04](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-ietf-hpke-pq-04#name-asymmetric-key-authenticate)). It would be nice to be able to check if such modes are supported for a suite.

Therefore I propose adding a function returning a boolean that indicates if a specific mode is supported:
~~~c
int OSSL_HPKE_mode_is_supported(OSSL_HPKE_SUITE suite, int mode);
~~~

### Can't convert suite to string

Currently one can convert a string into a suite, but not the other way around. While not a functional restraint, I suspect such functionality could be useful (e.g. for debugging purposes), so I suggest adding a function that returns a normalized string describing the suite:

~~~c
char* OSSL_HPKE_suite2str(OSSL_HPKE_SUITE suite);
~~~

labels.description: The issue was opened to request a feature
labels.name: issue: feature request

The macros referencing them were removed in 2015 in apps/ca.c, at the same time as the crl_dir macro removal: It would probably be less confusing to remove them from the config files, such as in openssl.cnf: The TSA section certs entry is still in use in ts_conf.c (ENV_CERTS) and should not be removed.

Please remember to tell us in what OpenSSL version you found the issue.

For build issues:

    If this is a build issue, please include the configuration output
    as well as a log of all errors.  Don't forget to include the exact
    commands you typed.

    With OpenSSL before 1.1.1, the configuration output comes from the
    configuration command.  With OpenSSL 1.1.1 and on, it's the output
    of `perl configdata.pm --dump`

For other issues:

    If it isn't a build issue, example code or commands to reproduce
    the issue is highly appreciated.
    Also, please remember to tell us if you worked with your own
    OpenSSL build or if it is system provided.

Please remember to put ``` lines before and after any commands plus
output and code, like this:

    ```
    $ echo output output output
    output output output
    ```

items.title: BUG

items.body:
## Description

When `openssl s_server` is run with `-www` or `-WWW`, a TLS 1.3 client that
sends `KeyUpdate(request_update=update_requested)` appears to receive one or
more `NewSessionTicket` messages before receiving the required
`KeyUpdate(update_not_requested)` response. In the observed reproduction, the
KeyUpdate response is never sent.

This appears specific to `-www`/`-WWW` mode, where `SSL_new_session_ticket()`
is called proactively while handling application data. Standard `s_server`
without `-www` did not reproduce the issue in my testing.

RFC 8446 Section 4.6.3 states:

> If the request_update field is set to "update_requested", then the receiver
> **MUST** send a KeyUpdate of its own with request_update set to
> "update_not_requested" **prior to sending its next Application Data record**.

Related: #8677 is about intolerance to KeyUpdate during pending writes; this
issue is about the *wrong message type* being sent as the response.

## Affected Versions

- **Confirmed**: OpenSSL 3.1.8 (`openssl-3.1.8-r1` on alpine:3.19)
- **Potentially affected**: other 3.x branches where `SSL_new_session_ticket()`
  is used proactively by `-www`/`-WWW` mode, or by any application that calls
  `SSL_new_session_ticket()` while application data is in flight (not
  independently verified on other branches)

## Steps to Reproduce

**Terminal 1 - start server with `-www`**:
```bash
openssl req -x509 -newkey rsa:2048 -keyout /tmp/srv.key -out /tmp/srv.crt \
  -days 1 -nodes -subj "/CN=test"
openssl s_server -tls1_3 -www -accept 0.0.0.0:4433 \
  -key /tmp/srv.key -cert /tmp/srv.crt
```

**Terminal 2 - connect, call `SSL_key_update`, observe server response**:

```python
# repro_keyupdate.py  (requires: py3-cffi, libssl.so.3)
import cffi, socket, time, struct

ffi = cffi.FFI()
ffi.cdef("""
typedef struct ssl_ctx_st SSL_CTX;
typedef struct ssl_st SSL;
typedef struct ssl_method_st SSL_METHOD;
const SSL_METHOD *TLS_client_method(void);
SSL_CTX *SSL_CTX_new(const SSL_METHOD *meth);
void     SSL_CTX_free(SSL_CTX *ctx);
void     SSL_CTX_set_verify(SSL_CTX *ctx, int mode, void *cb);
long     SSL_CTX_ctrl(SSL_CTX *ctx, int cmd, long larg, void *parg);
SSL     *SSL_new(SSL_CTX *ctx);
void     SSL_free(SSL *ssl);
int      SSL_set_fd(SSL *ssl, int fd);
int      SSL_connect(SSL *ssl);
int      SSL_write(SSL *ssl, const void *buf, int num);
int      SSL_key_update(SSL *ssl, int updatetype);
int      SSL_shutdown(SSL *ssl);
""")
libssl = ffi.dlopen("libssl.so.3")

ctx = libssl.SSL_CTX_new(libssl.TLS_client_method())
libssl.SSL_CTX_set_verify(ctx, 0, ffi.NULL)
libssl.SSL_CTX_ctrl(ctx, 123, 0x0304, ffi.NULL)  # set_min_proto_version TLS1.3
libssl.SSL_CTX_ctrl(ctx, 124, 0x0304, ffi.NULL)  # set_max_proto_version TLS1.3

raw = socket.create_connection(("127.0.0.1", 4433))
raw.setblocking(True)
ssl = libssl.SSL_new(ctx)
libssl.SSL_set_fd(ssl, raw.fileno())
assert libssl.SSL_connect(ssl) == 1
print("handshake done")
time.sleep(0.3)  # let server push its NewSessionTickets

# Send KeyUpdate(update_requested) - server MUST respond with KeyUpdate(update_not_requested)
libssl.SSL_key_update(ssl, 1)   # 1 = SSL_KEY_UPDATE_REQUESTED
libssl.SSL_write(ssl, b" ", 1)  # flush: OpenSSL sends queued KeyUpdate before app data
time.sleep(0.5)

# Read raw records - size is used as a heuristic to distinguish types
# (TLS 1.3 encrypts all post-handshake records; exact type requires decryption):
#   KeyUpdate ciphertext ~ 22 bytes  (5-byte plaintext + 1 inner type + 16 tag)
#   NewSessionTicket    ~ 250 bytes  (~233-byte plaintext + overhead)
# tls-anvil independently confirms the actual message types (see below).
raw.settimeout(2)
chunks = b""
try:
    while True:
        c = raw.recv(16384)
        if not c: break
        chunks += c
except: pass

libssl.SSL_shutdown(ssl)
raw.close()

i, ku, nst = 0, 0, 0
while i + 5 <= len(chunks):
    ct = chunks[i]; ln = (chunks[i+3] << 8) | chunks[i+4]
    if ct == 0x17:
        if 18 <= ln <= 30:  ku  += 1
        elif ln > 150:      nst += 1
    i += 5 + ln

print(f"KeyUpdate responses : {ku}")
print(f"NewSessionTickets   : {nst}")
if ku and not nst:   print("PASS")
elif nst and not ku: print("FAIL - RFC 8446 Section 4.6.3 violated")
else:                print(f"INCONCLUSIVE (ku={ku} nst={nst})")
```

**Expected**:
```
handshake done
KeyUpdate responses : 1
NewSessionTickets   : 0
PASS
```

**Actual** (OpenSSL 3.1.8, `s_server -tls1_3 -www`):
```
handshake done
KeyUpdate responses : 0
NewSessionTickets   : 2
FAIL - RFC 8446 Section 4.6.3 violated
```

### Reproduction with tls-anvil

Running [tls-anvil](https://github.com/tls-attacker/TLS-Anvil) against
`openssl s_server -tls1_3 -www` (strength=1):

```
Test:   TLS13.KeyUpdate.respondsWithValidKeyUpdate
Result: FULLY_FAILED  (18/18 parameter combinations)

Test:   TLS13.KeyUpdate.appDataUnderNewKeysSucceeds
Result: FULLY_FAILED  (20/20 parameter combinations)

AdditionalResultInformation:
  "Failed ReceiveAction: | Expected: KEY_UPDATE | Actual: NEW_SESSION_TICKET, NEW_SESSION_TICKET |"
```

Every parameter combination fails - the issue is not parameter-dependent.

## Root Cause Analysis (Hypothesis)

The write-state machine in `ssl/statem/statem_srvr.c` (around line 597) already
checks `s->key_update` *before* `s->ext.extra_tickets_expected`, which should
correctly prioritise KeyUpdate:

```c
case TLS_ST_OK:
    if (s->key_update != SSL_KEY_UPDATE_NONE) {
        st->hand_state = TLS_ST_SW_KEY_UPDATE;   // should fire first
        return WRITE_TRAN_CONTINUE;
    }
    // ...
    if (s->ext.extra_tickets_expected > 0) {
        st->hand_state = TLS_ST_SW_SESSION_TICKET;
        return WRITE_TRAN_CONTINUE;
    }
```

The hypothesis is that in `-www` mode, `SSL_new_session_ticket()` is called
proactively on every GET request. This call both sets
`extra_tickets_expected > 0` **and** appears to directly transition the state
machine to `TLS_ST_SW_SESSION_TICKET` before `tls_process_key_update()` has a
chance to set `s->key_update`.

A possible contributing factor is the coordination point in
`ssl/record/rec_layer_s3.c` (around line 318):

```c
if (s->rlayer.wpend_tot == 0 &&
    (s->key_update != SSL_KEY_UPDATE_NONE ||
     s->ext.extra_tickets_expected > 0))
    ossl_statem_set_in_init(s, 1);
```

which evaluates both conditions simultaneously. If a write is already in
progress (`wpend_tot > 0`), ordering may not be guaranteed, and `s->key_update`
may be lost or cleared by the time the state machine re-enters `TLS_ST_OK`.

This analysis is based on code inspection and reproduction results; I have not
confirmed the exact failure path with a debugger. Happy to provide additional
data if that would help narrow it down.
items.title: s_server -www sends NewSessionTicket instead of required KeyUpdate response after client KeyUpdate request

items.body:
## Description

RFC 8446 Section 4.1.2 requires:

> For every TLS 1.3 ClientHello, this vector MUST contain exactly one byte,
> set to zero, which corresponds to the "null" compression method in prior
> versions of TLS. If a TLS 1.3 ClientHello is received with any other value
> in this field, the server **MUST** abort the handshake with an
> "illegal_parameter" alert.

OpenSSL 3.1.8 does not consistently enforce this requirement. Under a specific
parameter combination (non-null `compression_methods`, FFDHE3072 key share,
ClientHello fragmented into 50-byte TLS records, with several optional
extensions present), the server keeps the socket open without sending the
required `illegal_parameter` alert.

Issue #6600 reported the same violation against master/1.1.1 and was closed as
fixed. This report documents a recurrence (or incomplete fix) in the 3.1.x
branch.

## Affected Versions

- **Confirmed**: OpenSSL 3.1.8 (`openssl-3.1.8-r1` on alpine:3.19)
- **Claimed fixed**: >= 1.1.1 (per #6600 resolution)

## Steps to Reproduce

```bash
# Terminal 1: start TLS 1.3-only server
openssl req -x509 -newkey rsa:2048 -keyout /tmp/srv.key -out /tmp/srv.crt \
  -days 1 -nodes -subj "/CN=test"
openssl s_server -tls1_3 -accept 0.0.0.0:4433 -key /tmp/srv.key -cert /tmp/srv.crt
```

```python
# Terminal 2: repro_compression.py  (requires: python3)
# Sends a TLS 1.3 ClientHello with compression_methods=[DEFLATE,null] fragmented
# into 50-byte TLS records, with FFDHE3072 key share and optional extensions.
# This is the exact parameter combination that triggers the bug.
import socket, struct, os, time, select, subprocess, tempfile

def u16(n): return struct.pack('>H', n)
def u8(n):  return struct.pack('B', n)

GREASE = b'\x0a\x0a'
random_bytes = os.urandom(32)
session_id   = os.urandom(32)

cipher_suites = GREASE + b'\x13\x01'                    # TLS_AES_128_GCM_SHA256
compression_methods = bytes([0x02, 0x01, 0x00])         # [DEFLATE, null] -- invalid for TLS 1.3

exts = b''
exts += struct.pack('>HHB', 0x002b, 3, 2) + b'\x03\x04'   # supported_versions: TLS 1.3
groups = GREASE + b'\x01\x01\x00\x1d\x00\x17'
exts += struct.pack('>HH', 0x000a, 2+len(groups)) + u16(len(groups)) + groups  # supported_groups
ffdhe3072_pub = os.urandom(384)                          # 384-byte FFDHE3072 public key
ks = b'\x01\x01' + u16(384) + ffdhe3072_pub
exts += struct.pack('>HH', 0x0033, 2+len(ks)) + u16(len(ks)) + ks             # key_share
sig = GREASE + b'\x04\x03\x05\x03\x06\x03\x08\x07\x08\x08\x04\x01\x05\x01\x06\x01'
exts += struct.pack('>HH', 0x000d, 2+len(sig)) + u16(len(sig)) + sig          # sig_algs
exts += struct.pack('>HHB', 0x002d, 2, 1) + b'\x01'    # psk_key_exchange_modes
exts += struct.pack('>HH', 0x0017, 0)                   # extended_master_secret
exts += struct.pack('>HH', 0x0023, 0)                   # session_ticket
exts += struct.pack('>HHB', 0xff01, 1, 0)               # renegotiation_info
alpn = b'\x08http/1.1'
exts += struct.pack('>HH', 0x0010, 4+len(alpn)) + u16(2+len(alpn)) + u16(len(alpn)) + alpn
exts += struct.pack('>HHB', 0x000f, 1, 1)               # heartbeat
exts += struct.pack('>HHB', 0x0001, 1, 2)               # max_fragment_length: 512
pad = max(0, 600 - (4+32+1+32+2+len(cipher_suites)+len(compression_methods)+2+len(exts)) - 4)
exts += struct.pack('>HH', 0x0015, pad) + b'\x00'*pad   # padding

body = (b'\x03\x03' + random_bytes + u8(len(session_id)) + session_id +
        u16(len(cipher_suites)) + cipher_suites + compression_methods +
        u16(len(exts)) + exts)
handshake = b'\x01' + struct.pack('>I', len(body))[1:] + body

# Fragment into 50-byte TLS records -- this is the key trigger
wire = b''
for i in range(0, len(handshake), 50):
    c = handshake[i:i+50]
    wire += b'\x16\x03\x01' + u16(len(c)) + c
wire += b'\x14\x03\x03\x00\x01\x01'                    # compat CCS

s = socket.create_connection(('127.0.0.1', 4433))
s.sendall(wire)

chunks = b''
deadline = time.time() + 4
while time.time() < deadline:
    r, _, _ = select.select([s], [], [], 0.2)
    if r:
        try:
            d = s.recv(4096)
            if not d: break
            chunks += d
            if chunks[0] == 0x15: break
        except OSError: break

s.close()
if not chunks:
    print("FAIL: no response -- socket stayed open, no alert sent")
elif chunks[0] == 0x15 and len(chunks) >= 7:
    lvl, desc = chunks[5], chunks[6]
    print(f"Alert: level={lvl} desc={desc}")
    print("PASS" if lvl == 2 and desc == 47 else "UNEXPECTED alert")
else:
    print(f"Other: {chunks[:8].hex()}")
```

**Expected**:
```
Alert: level=2 desc=47
PASS
```

**Actual** (OpenSSL 3.1.8, `s_server -tls1_3`):
```
FAIL: no response -- socket stayed open, no alert sent
```

### Reproduction with tls-anvil (exhaustive)

[tls-anvil](https://github.com/tls-attacker/TLS-Anvil) against
`openssl s_server -tls1_3` (strength=1):

```
Test:   TLS13.ClientHello.invalidCompression
Result: PARTIALLY_FAILED
        1 out of 11 parameter combinations -> socket still open, no alert sent
        Remaining 10/11 -> correct illegal_parameter(47) alert

Failure message:
  "Expected a fatal alert but no messages have been received and socket is still open"

Failing combination:
  CIPHER_SUITE=TLS_AES_128_GCM_SHA256, NAMED_GROUP=FFDHE3072,
  RECORD_LENGTH=50, MAX_FRAGMENT_LENGTH=TWO_9, all optional extensions enabled
```

## Code Location (Hypothesis)

The check exists in `ssl/statem/statem_srvr.c`:

```c
if (SSL_CONNECTION_IS_TLS13(s)) {
    if (clienthello->compressions_len != 1) {
        SSLfatal(s, SSL_AD_ILLEGAL_PARAMETER,
            SSL_R_INVALID_COMPRESSION_ALGORITHM);
        goto err;
    }
}
```

The hypothesis is that `SSL_CONNECTION_IS_TLS13(s)` at this point in the
parsing flow depends on `supported_versions` having already been parsed and the
TLS 1.3 version confirmed. When the ClientHello is heavily fragmented (50-byte
records) and contains many extensions, the version identity may not yet be
established when this check runs, causing the guard to evaluate as false and
the TLS 1.2 path (which permits non-null compression) to be taken instead.

This analysis is based on code inspection; the exact failing condition was
identified via tls-anvil exhaustive testing rather than a debugger trace.
items.title: TLS 1.3 server does not reliably reject ClientHello with non-null compression methods (possible regression of %236600)

items.body:
## Description

RFC 8446 Section 4.1.2 specifies that a TLS 1.3 ClientHello MUST contain either:
- a non-empty `key_share` extension, **or**
- a `pre_shared_key` extension with PSK key exchange modes that include `psk_dhe_ke`

When a ClientHello arrives with `supported_versions=0x0304` but without a
`key_share` extension and without any `pre_shared_key` extension, the server
must respond with a fatal alert (or at minimum a `HelloRetryRequest`). It must
not keep the socket open without responding.

OpenSSL 3.1.8 appears to violate this requirement under a specific parameter
combination: when the ClientHello is fragmented across many small TLS records
(RECORD_LENGTH=50) and contains several optional extensions, the server keeps
the socket open indefinitely without sending any alert.

The same applies to `supported_groups`: RFC 8446 requires the extension
whenever (EC)DHE is in use. OpenSSL's check for a missing `supported_groups`
is embedded inside `tls_parse_ctos_key_share()`, so if `key_share` is absent
the `supported_groups` check is never reached either. This is also reproducible
with the same fragmentation approach.

## Affected Versions

- **Confirmed**: OpenSSL 3.1.8 (`openssl-3.1.8-r1` on alpine:3.19)

## Steps to Reproduce

```bash
# Terminal 1: TLS 1.3-only server, no PSK configured
openssl req -x509 -newkey rsa:2048 -keyout /tmp/srv.key -out /tmp/srv.crt \
  -days 1 -nodes -subj "/CN=test"
openssl s_server -tls1_3 -accept 0.0.0.0:4433 -key /tmp/srv.key -cert /tmp/srv.crt
```

```python
# Terminal 2: repro_missing_keyshare.py  (requires: python3)
# Tests two cases identified from tls-anvil exhaustive results:
#   omitKeyShare:        no key_share + no supported_groups, RECORD_LENGTH=50
#   omitSupportedGroups: key_share present (FFDHE2048) but no supported_groups, RECORD_LENGTH=50
import socket, struct, os, time, select

def u16(n): return struct.pack('>H', n)
def u8(n):  return struct.pack('B', n)
GREASE = b'\x0a\x0a'

def send_and_check(handshake, record_len, label):
    wire = b''
    for i in range(0, len(handshake), record_len):
        c = handshake[i:i+record_len]
        wire += b'\x16\x03\x01' + u16(len(c)) + c
    wire += b'\x14\x03\x03\x00\x01\x01'   # compat CCS
    s = socket.create_connection(('127.0.0.1', 4433))
    s.sendall(wire)
    chunks = b''
    deadline = time.time() + 4
    while time.time() < deadline:
        r, _, _ = select.select([s], [], [], 0.2)
        if r:
            try:
                d = s.recv(4096)
                if not d: break
                chunks += d
                if chunks[0] == 0x15: break
            except OSError: break
    s.close()
    if not chunks:
        print(f"{label}: FAIL -- socket stayed open, no alert (RFC 8446 violated)")
    elif chunks[0] == 0x15 and len(chunks) >= 7:
        lvl, desc = chunks[5], chunks[6]
        ok = lvl == 2 and desc in (40, 47, 109)
        print(f"{label}: {'PASS' if ok else 'UNEXPECTED'} -- Alert level={lvl} desc={desc}")
    else:
        print(f"{label}: OTHER -- {chunks[:8].hex()}")

def build_exts(include_ks=True, include_sg=True, named_grp=b'\x00\x1d', pubkey=None):
    e = b''
    e += struct.pack('>HHB', 0x002b, 3, 2) + b'\x03\x04'   # supported_versions
    if include_sg:
        grps = GREASE + named_grp + b'\x00\x1d\x00\x17'
        e += struct.pack('>HH', 0x000a, 2+len(grps)) + u16(len(grps)) + grps
    if include_ks:
        pk = pubkey or os.urandom(32)
        ks = named_grp + u16(len(pk)) + pk
        e += struct.pack('>HH', 0x0033, 2+len(ks)) + u16(len(ks)) + ks
    sig = GREASE + b'\x04\x03\x05\x03\x06\x03\x08\x07\x08\x04\x01\x05\x01\x06\x01'
    e += struct.pack('>HH', 0x000d, 2+len(sig)) + u16(len(sig)) + sig
    e += struct.pack('>HHB', 0x002d, 2, 1) + b'\x01'
    e += struct.pack('>HH', 0x0017, 0)
    e += struct.pack('>HH', 0x0023, 0)
    e += struct.pack('>HHB', 0xff01, 1, 0)
    alpn = b'\x08http/1.1'
    e += struct.pack('>HH', 0x0010, 4+len(alpn)) + u16(2+len(alpn)) + u16(len(alpn)) + alpn
    e += struct.pack('>HHB', 0x000f, 1, 1)
    e += struct.pack('>HHB', 0x0001, 1, 2)
    pad = max(0, 500 - len(e) - 4)
    e += struct.pack('>HH', 0x0015, pad) + b'\x00'*pad
    return e

def build_hello(exts):
    r, sid = os.urandom(32), os.urandom(32)
    cs = GREASE + b'\x13\x01'
    body = b'\x03\x03' + r + u8(32) + sid + u16(len(cs)) + cs + b'\x01\x00' + u16(len(exts)) + exts
    return b'\x01' + struct.pack('>I', len(body))[1:] + body

# Case 1: omitKeyShare -- no key_share, no supported_groups
send_and_check(build_hello(build_exts(include_ks=False, include_sg=False)), 50, "omitKeyShare")

# Case 2: omitSupportedGroups -- FFDHE2048 key_share present, no supported_groups
send_and_check(build_hello(build_exts(include_ks=True, include_sg=False,
    named_grp=b'\x01\x00', pubkey=os.urandom(256))), 50, "omitSupportedGroups")
```

**Expected**:
```
omitKeyShare:        PASS -- Alert level=2 desc=109
omitSupportedGroups: PASS -- Alert level=2 desc=109
```

**Actual** (OpenSSL 3.1.8, `s_server -tls1_3`):
```
omitKeyShare:        FAIL -- socket stayed open, no alert (RFC 8446 violated)
omitSupportedGroups: FAIL -- socket stayed open, no alert (RFC 8446 violated)
```

### Reproduction with tls-anvil (exhaustive)

[tls-anvil](https://github.com/tls-attacker/TLS-Anvil) against
`openssl s_server -tls1_3` (no PSK configured, strength=1):

```
Test:   TLS13.ClientHello.omitKeyShare
Result: PARTIALLY_FAILED
        6 out of 22 parameter combinations -> socket still open, no alert
        16/22 -> correct fatal alert

Test:   TLS13.ClientHello.omitSupportedGroups
Result: PARTIALLY_FAILED
        10 out of 25 parameter combinations -> socket still open, no alert
        15/25 -> correct fatal alert

Failure message (both tests):
  "Expected a fatal alert but no messages have been received and socket is still open"

Failing combination examples:
  - CIPHER=TLS_AES_128_GCM_SHA256, RECORD_LENGTH=50,  all optional exts enabled
  - CIPHER=TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384, RECORD_LENGTH=111, all optional exts enabled
  - CIPHER=TLS_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256, RECORD_LENGTH=1
```

## Root Cause (Hypothesis)

### omitKeyShare

The final validation is in `ssl/statem/extensions.c`, `final_key_share()`:

```c
/* server side, key_share extension was not sent by client */
if (s->server && !sent) {
    if ((s->ext.psk_kex_mode & TLSEXT_KEX_MODE_FLAG_KE) != 0) {
        /* PSK-only mode: key_share is optional -- skip further checks */
        return 1;
    }
    /* falls through to HRR logic, which does not always send a fatal alert */
}
```

The hypothesis is that when no `pre_shared_key` extension is present,
`psk_kex_mode` is initialised to a default that includes
`TLSEXT_KEX_MODE_FLAG_KE`. The PSK-only bypass is therefore taken even though
no PSK was actually offered. For specific parameter combinations (notably when
the ClientHello is heavily fragmented) the server then neither sends HRR nor a
fatal alert, leaving the socket open.

### omitSupportedGroups

The `supported_groups` presence check for TLS 1.3 lives inside
`tls_parse_ctos_key_share()` in `ssl/statem/extensions_srvr.c` (~line 870):

```c
if (clnt_num_groups == 0) {
    SSLfatal(s, SSL_AD_MISSING_EXTENSION,
             SSL_R_MISSING_SUPPORTED_GROUPS_EXTENSION);
    return 0;
}
```

This function is only invoked when the `key_share` extension is present. When
`key_share` is absent, `tls_parse_ctos_key_share()` is never called and the
`supported_groups` absence goes undetected. There does not appear to be an
independent `supported_groups` presence check for the TLS 1.3 server path.

This analysis is based on code inspection and tls-anvil results; the exact
failing code path was not confirmed with a debugger trace.
items.title: TLS 1.3 server silently accepts ClientHello missing required key_share / supported_groups extensions when PSK is not offered

items.title: Possible integer overflow in KDF buffer sizing in tls1_export_keying_material()

items.body:
In the course of migrating from QuicTLS to OpenSSL's native QUIC integration, we've found a serious functionality difference. OpenSSL registers callbacks that send for each CRYPTO frame that the underlying transport must deliver, but does not have a callback to signal that the flight is done and the frames should be flushed out in a QUIC packet. This creates an architectural challenge as either we aggressively send each frame in a packet, or have some other event to trigger sending that will come too late (or try to insert higher level calls which isn't feasible in an application the size of Akamai's main serving application).

Thankfully dispatch tables are extensible, so this can be fixed in a backwards compatible way. We can prepare a patch but it will be some time before we get to it.
items.title: SSL_set_quic_tls_cbs() does not have a Flush Flight Callback equivalent

Problem Description
----

While trying to statically link libcrypto.a into a large PowerPC 64-bit application, we are getting relocation overflow errors:
```
$ gcc reproducer.o openssl-3.5.1/lib64/libcrypto.a -o test -ldl -pthread -lz
/tmp/ccdTi9n5.ltrans1.ltrans.o: in function `OPENSSL_cpuid_setup':
<artificial>:(.text.startup+0x350): relocation truncated to fit: R_PPC64_REL24 against symbol `OPENSSL_rdtsc_mftb' defined in .text section in /home/dante/openssl-3.5.1/lib64/libcrypto.a(libcrypto-lib-ppccpuid.o)
<artificial>:(.text.startup+0x388): relocation truncated to fit: R_PPC64_REL24 against symbol `OPENSSL_rdtsc_mfspr268' defined in .text section in /home/dante/openssl-3.5.1/lib64/libcrypto.a(libcrypto-lib-ppccpuid.o)
collect2: error: ld returned 1 exit status
```

### OS Name, Version, Hardware platform
```
Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8.4 (Ootpa)
Platform: ppc64le
```


How to reproduce
----
reproducer.c:
```
#include <openssl/crypto.h>
#include <openssl/evp.h>

__asm__(
  ".pushsection .text,\"ax\",@progbits\n"
  ".balign 16\n"

  "text_gap_end:\n"
  ".popsection\n"
);

void OPENSSL_cpuid_setup(void) __attribute__((longcall));

int main() {
  return EVP_MD_get_size(EVP_sha256()) == 32 ? 0 : 1;
}

gcc reproducer.o openssl-3.5.1/lib64/libcrypto.a -o test -ldl -pthread -lz

```
diff --git a/crypto/ppccap.c b/crypto/ppccap.c
index 08794a3ba6..b8ecd68793 100644
--- a/crypto/ppccap.c
+++ b/crypto/ppccap.c
@@ -137,6 +137,7 @@ static unsigned long getauxval(unsigned long key)
 
 # if defined(__GNUC__) && __GNUC__>=2
 __attribute__ ((constructor))
+__attribute__((section(".text")))
 # endif
 void OPENSSL_cpuid_setup(void)
 {
```

I get a BIO if I use the same file for passin and passout:

```
$ openssl pkcs12 -export -inkey PrivateKey.pem -in Certificate.pem -certfile CertificateChain.pem -out out.p12 -passout file:pass.txt -passin file:pass.txt
Error reading password from BIO
Error getting passwords

$ export pp=$(cat pass.txt)

$ openssl pkcs12 -export -inkey PrivateKey.pem -in Certificate.pem -certfile CertificateChain.pem -out out.p12 -passout file:pass.txt -passin env:pp
```
at a stable Gentoo Linux with OpenSSL 3.5.6

items.body:
# Bug Report: AES-256-CBC decryption fails on Zhaoxin KX-7000 CPU with OpenSSL 3.5.x

## Environment

| Item | Details |
|------|---------|
| OS | Debian 13 (Trixie), kernel 6.12.88+deb13-amd64 |
| CPU (broken) | Zhaoxin KaiXian KX-7000 |
| OpenSSL (broken) | 3.5.6 |
| OpenSSL (working, same CPU) | 3.2.4 (UOS V25) |
| Node.js | v22.22.3 |

## Description

AES-256-CBC decryption consistently fails with `bad decrypt` on Zhaoxin KX-7000 CPU when using OpenSSL 3.5.6. The **exact same test** passes on:
- Zhaoxin KX-7000 + OpenSSL **3.2.4**
- Intel Celeron J1900 + OpenSSL **3.5.6**

This confirms the bug is a regression specific to **Zhaoxin KX-7000's AES-NI implementation** triggered by changes introduced between OpenSSL 3.2 and 3.5.

AES-256-GCM works correctly on all tested combinations.

## Reproduction

```javascript
const crypto = require('crypto');

const key = crypto.randomBytes(32);
const iv = crypto.randomBytes(16);

// Encryption succeeds
const cipher = crypto.createCipheriv('aes-256-cbc', key, iv);
let encrypted = cipher.update('Hello Pylance!', 'utf8', 'hex');
encrypted += cipher.final('hex');
console.log('Encrypt OK:', encrypted);

// Decryption FAILS on Zhaoxin KX-7000 + OpenSSL 3.5.6
const decipher = crypto.createDecipheriv('aes-256-cbc', key, iv);
let decrypted = decipher.update(encrypted, 'hex', 'utf8');
try {
  decrypted += decipher.final('utf8');
  console.log('Decrypt OK:', decrypted);
} catch (e) {
  console.log('Decrypt FAILED:', e.code, e.message);
  // Error: error:03000064:digital envelope routines::bad decrypt
  //   at Decipheriv.final (node:internal/crypto/cipher:184:29)
}
```

## Test Results Matrix

| CPU | OpenSSL | AES-256-CBC Encrypt | AES-256-CBC Decrypt | AES-256-GCM |
|-----|---------|--------------------|--------------------|-------------|
| Zhaoxin KX-7000 | 3.2.4 | ✅ OK | ✅ **OK** | ✅ OK |
| Zhaoxin KX-7000 | 3.5.6 | ✅ OK | ❌ **FAIL** | ✅ OK |
| Intel Celeron J1900 | 3.5.6 | ✅ OK | ✅ **OK** | ✅ OK |

> **Key finding**: Intel J1900 and Zhaoxin KX-7000 run the **same Node.js v22.22.3 + OpenSSL 3.5.6** binary,
> but only Zhaoxin KX-7000 fails. This rules out any software version difference and points directly to
> Zhaoxin's AES-NI hardware behavior with the optimized CBC decryption path introduced in OpenSSL 3.3–3.5.

## Error Output (Zhaoxin KX-7000 + OpenSSL 3.5.6)

```
=== 系统信息 ===
Platform: linux x64
CPU: ZHAOXIN KaiXian KX-7000
Node: v22.22.3
OpenSSL: 3.5.6

=== AES-256-CBC ===
Encrypt OK: f0a4ce97fd67763499379c0289e7b919...
FAILED: error:03000064:digital envelope routines::bad decrypt

=== AES-256-GCM ===
Encrypt OK, tag length: 16
Decrypt OK: Pylance cache data with authentication

=== PBKDF2 ===
PBKDF2(10000): 3ms   ← works
PBKDF2(100000): 26ms ← works
```

## Reference Output (Intel Celeron J1900 + OpenSSL 3.5.6)

```
=== 系统信息 ===
Platform: linux x64
CPU: Intel(R) Celeron(R) CPU J1900 @ 1.99GHz
Node: v22.22.3
OpenSSL: 3.5.6

=== AES-256-CBC ===
Encrypt OK: c674f04f87806bde855875ec2522bd04...
Decrypt OK: Hello Pylance! 这是一个测试   ← passes on Intel

=== AES-256-GCM ===
Encrypt OK, tag length: 16
Decrypt OK: Pylance cache data with authentication
```

## Additional Notes

- `openssl speed -evp aes-256-cbc` benchmark runs without errors on Zhaoxin KX-7000 + OpenSSL 3.5.6, suggesting basic AES-NI instruction execution is functional
- Only the EVP `Decipheriv.final()` path fails; encryption always succeeds
- Failure is 100% reproducible — every single AES-256-CBC decryption attempt fails
- AES-256-GCM on the same hardware + OpenSSL version works perfectly
- Setting `OPENSSL_ia32cap` to disable hardware AES caused the affected application (VS Code Pylance) to silently fail entirely, suggesting the flag approach is not a viable workaround

## CPU Info

```
model name      : ZHAOXIN KaiXian KX-7000
OPENSSL_ia32cap : 0x7ffa73e3afcbffff:0x0040001d201c25ab:0x0000000024000000:0x0000000000000000:0x0000000000000000
```

## Hypothesis

The regression was introduced between OpenSSL 3.2 and 3.5, most likely related to:
- **VAES (Vector AES)** support added in OpenSSL 3.3
- **Optimized CBC decryption scheduling** changes in 3.4–3.5

Zhaoxin KX-7000 implements AES-NI but appears to have subtle behavioral differences from Intel/AMD in specific instruction sequences used by the newer CBC decryption path. The fact that GCM works and CBC encrypt works suggests the issue is isolated to the specific VAES/AES-NI instruction sequence used in the CBC **decryption** path.

## Suggested Investigation

1. Check if CBC decryption uses a different AES-NI instruction sequence than CBC encryption (e.g., `AESDEC` vs `AESENC` pipelining)
2. Check VAES (`VAESDEC`/`VAESDECLAST`) usage in the CBC path — Zhaoxin KX-7000's VAES support may have errata
3. Consider adding a CBC-decrypt self-test to `OPENSSL_cpuid_setup()` similar to how some implementations detect RDRAND bugs
4. A targeted workaround: disable VAES for CBC decrypt specifically when running on Zhaoxin CPUs (detectable via CPUID vendor string `CentaurHauls` / `  Shanghai  `)

## Workaround

Use `python:3.13-bookworm` (Debian 12, OpenSSL 3.1.x) as the runtime environment. OpenSSL 3.1.x is not affected.

items.body: In `win32_joiner()` (`crypto/dso/dso_win32.c`), the directory trailing-separator is budgeted into `len` only when `file_split->file` is non-NULL but is emitted unconditionally by the directory loop; when `dir` is set without a trailing separator and `file == NULL`, the final `result[offset] = '\0'` writes one byte past `OPENSSL_malloc(len + 1)`.

items.title: win32_joiner() in crypto/dso/dso_win32.c writes 1 byte past the allocation when the directory has no trailing separator and file is NULL

items.body: In `do_create()` (`crypto/asn1/asn_moid.c`), when an `oid_section` entry has an all-whitespace long-name field before the value separator, the forward `ln` cursor advances to the comma while the backward `p` cursor walks from comma-1; the `p == ln` guard never holds (the cursors moved in opposite directions), so `p` underflows below `value` for a heap out-of-bounds read.
items.title: do_create() in crypto/asn1/asn_moid.c reads below the input buffer when an oid_section entry has an all-whitespace long-name field
